title: What Is Somatic Therapy and How Does It Actually Work?
meta_description: Discover how somatic therapy helps heal trauma by connecting mind and body to release stored stress and restore emotional balance effectively.
og_title: What Is Somatic Therapy and How Does It Actually Work?
og_description: Discover how somatic therapy helps heal trauma by connecting mind and body to release stored stress and restore emotional balance effectively.
body:
Somatic therapy connects your mind and body to heal trauma. It works by tuning into physical sensations and nervous system states, not just thoughts. This isn't mystical. It's mechanical.

Look, here’s the thing. Trauma isn’t just a bad memory stuck in your brain. It’s a body story too. Ever felt a tight chest, a sinking stomach, or frozen limbs during stress? That’s not just anxiety. It’s trauma’s echo in your nervous system. Traditional talk therapy often misses that. Somatic therapy doesn’t. It works with your body to help you heal where words can’t reach.

What Exactly Is Somatic Therapy?

Somatic therapy is a form of treatment that focuses on the body’s role in trauma and emotional pain. Instead of just talking about what happened, it pays attention to how trauma lives in your body’s sensations, posture, and nervous system reactions. The name "somatic" means “of the body.” It's not about fancy energy work or spiritual mumbo jumbo. It’s about biology. Your brain and nervous system hold trauma in muscle tension, breath patterns, and even your heartbeat.

Peter Levine, one of the key pioneers of somatic therapy, explains in his book Waking the Tiger: Healing Trauma (paid link) that trauma is a physical experience stored in the nervous system. When you get stuck in fight, flight, or freeze, your body keeps holding on to that energy long after the danger is gone. Somatic therapy helps you discharge that energy safely, calming your nervous system and loosening that grip.

The Science Behind Somatic Healing

In my own practice, I’ve seen countless clients benefit from understanding the body’s role in trauma. It’s no surprise that modern neuroscience backs this up. Bessel van der Kolk’s work in The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) shows that trauma changes how your brain processes stress and memories. But it also rewires your body’s responses. Van der Kolk points out that trauma is stored in the body and can cause chronic health issues if left untreated.

Stephen Porges introduced the Polyvagal Theory, which explains how your vagus nerve controls your nervous system states—safe, mobilized, or immobilized. You’re not just anxious or calm; your nervous system is constantly shifting between these states. Somatic therapy tunes into these shifts and helps you reestablish a sense of safety at a biological level. If you want to geek out further, The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link) dives deep into this science.

How Does Somatic Therapy Work in Practice?

Stop overthinking this. Here’s what actually works: you learn to notice what’s happening inside your body in real-time. It might be a tightness in your throat, a quiver in your hands, or a blocked breath. Then, you slowly and intentionally shift those sensations, often with a therapist guiding you.

Pat Ogden, founder of Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, emphasizes combining movement, body awareness, and mindful attention to release trauma. It’s about reclaiming control over your body’s responses. You might engage in breathwork, gentle movement, or grounding exercises to reconnect with your body without getting overwhelmed.

Somatic therapy isn’t about forcing emotions to surface. It’s about creating a safe container for your nervous system to regulate. Babette Rothschild, another respected figure in trauma therapy, warns against pushing too hard too fast. Her approach teaches that trauma healing happens in small, digestible steps, respecting your nervous system’s pace.

Why The Body Holds Trauma—and Why It Matters

The body doesn’t lie. The mind does. Constantly. Your memories can trick you, but your body remembers every punch, every shock, every freezing moment. Resmaa Menakem calls trauma "white body trauma", describing how trauma affects not just the mind but muscle and tissue, especially in communities facing systemic violence.

When trauma remains unprocessed, it can show up as chronic pain, autoimmune diseases, or addiction. Bruce Perry’s research on childhood trauma reveals how early life stress rewires the brain and body, leading to long-term health struggles. Without addressing the somatic component, healing remains incomplete.

How You Can Start Using Somatic Tools Today

Less theory. More practice. You don’t need a therapist to start tuning into your body. Simple steps like noticing your breath or scanning for tension can begin to shift your nervous system. Try this now: pause and ask yourself, “What am I feeling in my body?”

Notice without judgment. Is your jaw clenched? Are your shoulders tight? This awareness helps you interrupt automatic stress patterns. If you want to dive deeper, check out Dan Siegel’s work. His concept of “mindsight” is about sensing your internal world and regulating it. It’s practical, not woo-woo.

For a hands-on approach, I recommend Anchored: How to Befriend Your Nervous System (paid link). It offers simple tools to calm your nervous system when trauma or stress flare up.

Over the years I’ve seen how these simple shifts make a huge difference. The body is always talking. You just have to listen.

Is somatic therapy only for people with extreme trauma?

No. While somatic therapy is well-known for treating PTSD and severe trauma, it’s helpful for anyone dealing with stress, anxiety, depression, or body-related issues like chronic pain. The body holds onto tension from everyday life too.

How is somatic therapy different from traditional talk therapy?

Traditional talk therapy focuses mainly on thoughts, beliefs, and narratives. Somatic therapy includes all that but puts a strong emphasis on physical sensations, breath, movement, and nervous system regulation. It’s about integrating body and mind.

Can I do somatic therapy on my own?

You can start with simple body awareness exercises, breathwork, or grounding techniques on your own. But because trauma can trigger intense reactions, working with a trained somatic therapist is safer for deeper work.

Somatic therapy is not some new-age fad. It’s grounded in decades of science and clinical evidence. When you start paying attention to your body’s signals, you’re tapping into a powerful healing resource. You don’t have to be stuck in your trauma story. You can rewrite it, one breath and one sensation at a time.

So here’s the challenge: next time you feel overwhelmed or stuck, don’t just think your way out. Check in with your body. What’s it telling you? The answers aren’t in your head. They’re in your nervous system.

Reviewed by The Oracle Lover, Intuitive Educator and Oracle Guide. Last updated 2026-05-05.

I've spent years writing about somatic healing on this site. The body's wisdom is not mystical - it's biological. And it's available to you right now.

title: Polyvagal Theory Explained: Why Your Nervous System Is Not Overreacting
meta_description: Discover how Polyvagal Theory explains your nervous system's response to trauma and learn effective somatic healing techniques to restore calm and safety.
og_title: Polyvagal Theory Explained: Why Your Nervous System Is Not Overreacting
og_description: Discover how Polyvagal Theory explains your nervous system's response to trauma and learn effective somatic healing techniques to restore calm and safety.
body:
Your nervous system is not overreacting. It’s doing exactly what it’s designed to do: keep you safe. Polyvagal Theory explains how your body processes safety and threat without judgment. Understanding this helps you stop blaming yourself and start healing.

Ever gotten irritated for no clear reason? Felt your heart race or your gut twist during what seemed like a harmless conversation? Look, here’s the thing: your nervous system isn’t malfunctioning or “too sensitive.” It’s working on a deep, biological level you rarely get to see. Polyvagal Theory reveals why your body reacts the way it does—and why those reactions are not signs of weakness or overreaction.

What Is Polyvagal Theory and Why Should You Care?

Developed by Stephen Porges over the last few decades, Polyvagal Theory redefines how we understand the autonomic nervous system (ANS). Forget the old “fight or flight” versus “rest and digest” model. Porges introduced a third branch: the social engagement system. This system helps you connect with others when you feel safe and signals your body to relax rather than react.

Here’s the science in a nutshell: your vagus nerve isn’t just one thing, it’s actually two distinct branches with different jobs. The dorsal vagal complex triggers shutdown or freeze responses, like when you “play dead” during extreme threat. The ventral vagal complex activates social engagement and calming states. Your body constantly shifts between these states based on perceived safety or danger.

Stop overthinking this. Your nervous system isn’t crazy. It’s trying to keep you alive. When it senses danger, even if that danger is just a harsh word or a tense room, it will react. You can’t blame yourself for that.

Why Your Nervous System Is Not Overreacting (It’s Just Doing Its Job)

Bessel van der Kolk, a giant in trauma research, points out in The Body Keeps the Score that trauma stays locked in the body. It’s stored in your nervous system’s automatic patterns. When your system detects even faint signals of past trauma, it can trigger intense bodily responses without your conscious control.

Think of your nervous system like a smoke detector. It’s designed to go off at the first hint of smoke, even if it’s a false alarm. Better safe than sorry. Your body isn’t “overreacting” when it goes into hypervigilance or freeze. It’s responding to cues from earlier experiences that you might not even remember consciously.

Dan Siegel, a leader in interpersonal neurobiology, emphasizes the importance of “attuned connection” to help regulate your nervous system. When you feel heard and safe, your ventral vagal system activates and calms you down. But if you’re stuck in survival mode, your body defaults to defensive strategies.

How Trauma Hijacks Your Nervous System

Peter Levine, author of Waking the Tiger, explains that trauma isn’t just in your head—it’s in your body’s automatic responses. Trauma changes how your nervous system perceives threat and safety. Even when danger is long gone, your system might keep you locked in patterns designed to protect you in the past.

Babette Rothschild adds that trauma essentially “rewires” your nervous system’s thresholds for arousal. This means your system might hit the red zone (fight, flight, or freeze) at much lower levels of stress than before. It’s not that you’re weak or making it up. It’s a biological fact of trauma.

Over the years I’ve seen how people misinterpret these automatic reactions as personal failings. The body doesn’t lie. The mind does. Constantly. Recognizing trauma’s impact on your nervous system is the first step toward reclaiming your sense of safety.

What You Can Do to Regulate Your Nervous System

Here’s what actually works: learn to notice your nervous system’s signals and respond with safety cues. Resmaa Menakem, author of My Grandmother’s Hands, teaches us that healing trauma is not just mental work. It’s about calming the body through breath, movement, and grounding.

Try simple practices that engage your ventral vagal system: slow, deep breathing, gentle eye contact with a trusted person, humming, or soft vocalizing. These are not woo-woo tricks. They’re activating your social engagement system, telling your body it’s safe to relax.

Bruce Perry advocates for “bottom-up” approaches that start with the body before the mind. This means working with movement, rhythm, and sensory experiences to downshift your defensive nervous system responses. The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy offers practical applications for therapists, but you can use many of these principles on your own.

Less theory. More practice. Find what brings your body into a window of tolerance—the sweet spot where you feel alert but relaxed. It might be yoga, walking barefoot, singing, or simply sitting quietly and noticing your breath.

Where to Go From Here

Understanding your nervous system’s responses through Polyvagal Theory isn’t about adding more labels or self-judgment. It’s about compassion. When your system flips into fight, flight, or freeze, remember it’s protecting you, not betraying you.

If you want to take a deeper dive, start with Stephen Porges’ work. Then explore trauma-informed resources like In an Unspoken Voice by Peter Levine. And remember, healing happens in the body first.

For further scientific reading, check out this NIH article on Polyvagal Theory and mental health.

Is Polyvagal Theory widely accepted in neuroscience?

Polyvagal Theory has gained significant attention and validation in trauma and psychotherapy fields. While some neuroscientists debate aspects of it, many clinicians find it a useful framework to understand and treat trauma. It’s a developing theory, but its core concepts about the vagus nerve’s role in safety and threat responses are well-supported.

Can I practice Polyvagal Theory techniques on my own?

Absolutely. Simple practices like slow breathing, gentle humming, and mindful grounding can activate your social engagement system. However, if you have severe trauma or dissociation, working with a trained therapist familiar with Polyvagal Theory is recommended.

Why do I sometimes feel frozen or numb in stressful situations?

This freeze or shutdown response is linked to the dorsal vagal branch of the vagus nerve. When fight or flight doesn’t feel possible or safe, your nervous system may default to immobilization to protect you. It’s a survival mechanism, not a personal flaw.

So, where does that leave you? Understanding your nervous system’s natural protective strategies can shift your perspective from self-blame to curiosity and care. The next time your body reacts strongly, remind yourself it’s not overreacting. It’s doing what it was built to do: keep you alive.

Now, take a breath. Feel your body. You’re not alone in this.

Reviewed by The Oracle Lover, Intuitive Educator and Oracle Guide. Last updated 2026-05-05.

title: The Window of Tolerance: What It Is and How to Widen Yours
meta_description: Learn what the window of tolerance is and discover effective somatic techniques to expand your emotional resilience and heal from trauma.
og_title: The Window of Tolerance: What It Is and How to Widen Yours
og_description: Learn what the window of tolerance is and discover effective somatic techniques to expand your emotional resilience and heal from trauma.
body:
Your nervous system has a sweet spot for handling stress. When you’re inside it, you function well. Outside it, you shut down or freak out. Widening this window means better emotional regulation and resilience.

Ever feel like your emotions are either flooding you or completely numb? Like you’re either wired tight with anxiety or checked out and disconnected? That’s your nervous system talking, and the space in between—the “Window of Tolerance”—is where you want to live. But most of us don’t know how to find it, let alone make it bigger. Here’s the no-BS guide to what this window really is and how to stretch it so life feels less like a roller coaster.

What Exactly Is the Window of Tolerance?

The term was coined by Dr. Dan Siegel, a clinical psychiatrist who studies trauma and the brain. The window of tolerance refers to the optimal zone of arousal where your nervous system can handle stress without going into fight-flight or freeze. Inside this window, you can think clearly, process emotions, and act with intention.

When you’re outside this window, two common extremes show up: hyperarousal and hypoarousal. Hyperarousal means you’re overwhelmed, anxious, or reactive. Hypoarousal means you’re numb, dissociated, or emotionally shut down. Neither state is sustainable or helpful in the long run.

Look, here’s the thing. Your window isn’t fixed. It can widen or shrink depending on your experiences, especially trauma. People with lots of unprocessed trauma tend to have narrow windows. This makes them vulnerable to emotional storms or shutdowns even when the trigger isn’t that big. The good news? You can train this window to get bigger over time.

Why Does This Window Matter So Much?

Dr. Bessel van der Kolk, author of The Body Keeps the Score, has shown how trauma lodges itself in the body and nervous system, constricting this window and sabotaging your ability to self-regulate. When your window is narrow, everyday stressors become unbearable, and you’re stuck bouncing between fight-flight or freeze modes.

Over the years I’ve seen many clients come in thinking their anxiety or numbness is a character flaw or weakness. It’s not. It’s biology reacting to what it’s been through. The body doesn’t lie. The mind does. Constantly. Understanding your window of tolerance gives you a map to rebuild stability and resilience.

What Happens When You’re Outside the Window?

Dr. Stephen Porges, who developed the Polyvagal Theory, explains how our autonomic nervous system has multiple pathways for responding to threat. When you’re hyperaroused, your sympathetic nervous system is in overdrive: heart races, muscles tense, brain floods with stress hormones. You’re ready to fight or flee.

On the flip side, hypoarousal means your parasympathetic system, specifically the dorsal vagal complex, has gone into a shutdown mode. Think freeze or collapse: numbness, dissociation, fatigue. Neither extreme lets you engage fully with life or solve problems effectively.

Pat Ogden, a pioneer in sensorimotor psychotherapy, highlights that trauma traps us in these states by disrupting our internal regulation. The goal of trauma healing is to bring the nervous system back into that window so you can feel safe in your body and your emotions.

How to Widen Your Window of Tolerance

Stop overthinking this. This isn’t mystical. It’s mechanical. You’re basically training your nervous system to tolerate bigger doses of discomfort without flipping out or shutting down. Here’s what actually works:

  1. Body Awareness Practices: Start noticing sensations in your body without judgment. Try grounding exercises like feeling your feet on the floor or slow breathing. Babette Rothschild recommends “tracking” sensations as a way to build tolerance in her book In an Unspoken Voice.
  2. Mindful Movement: Gentle yoga, tai chi, or simple stretches help regulate your nervous system by reconnecting you to your body in a safe way. Resmaa Menakem, author of My Grandmother’s Hands, stresses the importance of somatic awareness, especially for people who have experienced racial trauma.
  3. Controlled Exposure: Gradually facing small stressors while practicing self-soothing can expand your window. Bruce Perry’s research supports “progressive layering” of stress to build resilience.
  4. Therapeutic Support: Somatic therapies, EMDR, or Polyvagal-informed therapy can guide you safely through dysregulation and help widen your window. The book The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy is a solid resource if you want to understand how this works in practice.
  5. Self-Compassion & Patience: Remember, this isn’t a quick fix. Your nervous system changes slowly. Celebrate small gains and be kind to yourself when things feel hard.

Across the articles we’ve published on this site, the theme is clear: less theory, more practice. Knowing about the window of tolerance is only the first step. You need to give your body and brain repeated experiences of safety and regulation to stretch that window wider.

Where to Learn More and Get Support

For deeper understanding, the NIH article on Window of Tolerance and Emotion Regulation provides solid scientific grounding in this topic. It explains the neurobiological basis and offers insights into how trauma affects the brain and body.

If you want to dive into practical healing, the books I mentioned by van der Kolk, Levine, Rothschild, Menakem, and Perry are invaluable. They mix science with real-world tools that don’t rely on mystical explanations. You can get started with Waking the Tiger: Healing Trauma by Peter Levine for a clear somatic approach.

How do I know if I’m inside or outside my window of tolerance?

If you find yourself either flooded with anxiety, panic, irritability, or completely numb, disconnected, and zoned out, you’re likely outside your window. Inside it, you feel calm, grounded, and able to respond flexibly to stress.

Can trauma permanently shrink my window of tolerance?

Trauma can narrow your window by keeping your nervous system stuck in hyper- or hypoarousal. But the window is not permanently fixed. With consistent somatic work, therapy, and self-regulation techniques, you can expand it again.

What’s the quickest way to calm myself when I’m overwhelmed?

Simple grounding techniques like slow deep breathing, feeling your feet on the ground, or naming five things you see around you can shift your nervous system back toward the window temporarily. These don’t fix the underlying issue but help you get through the moment.

Final Thoughts

Learning about the window of tolerance is like getting a user manual for your nervous system. It explains why you sometimes lose it or shut down, and more importantly, how to take back control. You don’t have to be hostage to your emotional extremes. The key is to practice patience, build body awareness, and seek support where you need it. The window can widen. It must. Because inside that window is where you find your true power to live fully.

Reviewed by The Oracle Lover, Intuitive Educator and Oracle Guide. Last updated 2026-05-05.

title: Somatic Experiencing: Peter Levine's Approach to Releasing Stored Trauma
meta_description: Discover how Peter Levine's Somatic Experiencing method helps release stored trauma by using body awareness to heal and restore emotional balance.
og_title: Somatic Experiencing: Peter Levine's Approach to Releasing Stored Trauma
og_description: Discover how Peter Levine's Somatic Experiencing method helps release stored trauma by using body awareness to heal and restore emotional balance.
body:
Somatic Experiencing is a body-based approach to trauma healing developed by Peter Levine. It helps release trauma stored in the nervous system without reliving the event. This method focuses on physical sensations and nervous system regulation rather than just talk therapy.

What if the trauma you think you’ve processed is still trapped in your body? Not in your head where therapy sessions usually go, but deep down in your nervous system, quietly influencing your every move, your reactions, your sense of safety. Look, here’s the thing. Trauma isn’t just a memory stored in your brain. It’s a biological experience encoded in your body—and if you don’t address it there, it won’t fully let go.

What Is Somatic Experiencing? The Nuts and Bolts

Peter Levine came up with Somatic Experiencing (SE) after decades of studying trauma survivors and animal behavior. His core insight? Animals in the wild get triggered constantly, but they rarely develop chronic trauma symptoms because they instinctively “shake off” the trauma through physical movements. Humans, on the other hand, often get stuck in the freeze or shutdown response. Our nervous system doesn’t always complete its natural defensive response, leaving trauma frozen in the body.

SE works by helping you notice your physical sensations. That might mean a tightness in your chest, a buzzing in your gut, or even subtle shifts in posture. Instead of pushing to remember or talk through the trauma, you gently track these sensations. This tracking leads to a release of the stuck energy and restores your nervous system’s natural balance.

Here’s what actually works: slowing down, tracking sensations, and titrating—meaning you only touch the edge of the trauma instead of diving headfirst. This keeps you safe and prevents retraumatization.

Why The Body Holds Trauma, According to Science

Trauma affects your autonomic nervous system (ANS), which controls your fight, flight, freeze, and faint responses. Bessel van der Kolk, a leading trauma researcher, describes how trauma “hijacks” this system, making your body stay in a state of hyperarousal or numbness long after the event is over. This explains why trauma is often felt more than it’s remembered.

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory sheds light on how your nervous system’s different pathways influence your sense of safety. The vagus nerve, which Porges studies extensively, modulates your heart rate, breathing, and digestion—key players in trauma symptoms. Somatic Experiencing taps into this system by encouraging you to notice and shift autonomic states, leading to increased regulation and resilience.

In my own practice, I’ve seen clients move from constant anxiety or numbness to a state where their body feels alive and safe again—and it’s always about the nervous system, not just the story in their mind.

How Somatic Experiencing Differs From Traditional Talk Therapy

Most trauma therapy relies on narrative: recounting the trauma, understanding why it happened, and reframing your beliefs around it. While helpful, this approach can miss the core biological imprint trauma leaves behind. Babette Rothschild, an expert in trauma treatment, points out that trauma is stored in the body’s implicit memory, not just explicit memory (the facts you can describe).

Somatic Experiencing sidesteps the need to relive the event. Instead, it works by helping you renegotiate your trauma at a sensory level. No intense exposure necessary. This makes it safer, especially for those who get overwhelmed by traditional talk therapy.

Pat Ogden, who developed Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, echoes a similar view: body awareness is essential for trauma healing. SE is one of the clearest methods to cultivate this awareness without getting lost in analysis. Stop overthinking this. You don’t have to relive your worst moments to heal them.

Tools and Techniques: What Happens In a Somatic Experiencing Session?

Sessions usually begin with the practitioner guiding you to notice your internal sensations. You might be asked to focus on areas of tension, temperature changes, or subtle muscle twitches. The key is “felt sense” — the raw bodily experience that exists beneath your conscious thoughts.

Once you identify these sensations, the practitioner helps you track shifts. Maybe the tightness loosens or spreads. This process activates what Levine calls “pendulation”—the nervous system’s natural rhythm of moving between distress and safety. Pendulation allows your body to complete the defensive response it was never able to finish.

Another technique is “titration.” Instead of flooding your system with trauma, SE introduces small doses of distress that your nervous system can handle. This builds your capacity to regulate without shutting down or panicking.

If you want a deep dive into Levine’s approach, his classic book Waking the Tiger: Healing Trauma is a must-read (paid link).

Why Somatic Experiencing Is Not Mystical. It’s Biological

This isn’t mystical. It’s mechanical. The body doesn’t lie. The mind does. Constantly. When you learn to listen to your body’s signals, it’s like reading a clear map of what your nervous system is stuck on. The trauma gets “unstuck” when your nervous system completes the natural cycle of defense—freeze, fight, flight, or collapse.

Bruce Perry, a child trauma expert, emphasizes the importance of bottom-up healing, starting with the body and brainstem before moving to higher cognitive functions. Somatic Experiencing is designed precisely for that bottom-up approach.

Less theory. More practice. If you want to start experimenting on your own, you might consider Anchored: How to Befriend Your Nervous System (paid link). It offers practical exercises to calm and regulate your nervous system daily.

Trauma Is Not Your Identity. Healing Is Possible.

Across the articles we’ve published on this site, one message is clear: healing trauma isn’t about erasing your past. It’s about reclaiming your present. Somatic Experiencing gives you tools to feel safe in your body again. To move from survival mode into living mode.

Resmaa Menakem’s work on racialized trauma highlights the importance of body-centered healing in communities that often bear generational trauma. Trauma stored in bodies can be passed down, but it can also be released—if you know where and how to look.

If you want to understand the science behind trauma and why body-based therapies work, check out this NIH article on trauma and nervous system healing.

Is Somatic Experiencing suitable for all types of trauma?

SE is designed to address a wide range of trauma types, from acute incidents like accidents or assaults to complex, chronic trauma such as childhood neglect. However, it's important to work with a qualified practitioner to ensure safety and effectiveness, especially if trauma is severe or recent.

How many sessions of Somatic Experiencing are usually needed?

There’s no set number. Some people notice shifts after just a few sessions, while others may work with SE for months or longer. Trauma is complex, and progress depends on individual history and nervous system state.

Can I practice Somatic Experiencing on my own?

You can start by tuning into your body sensations, practicing grounding, and noticing your nervous system states. Books like Waking the Tiger or Anchored offer great starting points. But for deeper trauma work, working with a trained SE therapist is highly recommended.

Stop overthinking this. Healing trauma starts with feeling your body again. It’s not about “fixing” your mind or rewriting your story. It’s about completing what your nervous system couldn’t finish.

You have the capacity to heal. The tools are here. The body remembers. Now it’s time to listen.

title: EMDR Therapy: What It Is, What the Research Shows, and Who It Helps
meta_description: Discover how EMDR therapy aids somatic trauma healing, backed by research, and learn who can benefit from this effective treatment approach.
og_title: EMDR Therapy: What It Is, What the Research Shows, and Who It Helps
og_description: Discover how EMDR therapy aids somatic trauma healing, backed by research, and learn who can benefit from this effective treatment approach.
body:
EMDR helps rewire trauma memories through eye movements and bilateral stimulation. Research shows it’s effective for PTSD and other trauma-related issues. It works on how the brain processes pain, not by talking alone.

What if I told you trauma healing isn’t just about talking it out? You might think EMDR is some strange, mystical eye dance, but it’s not. It’s a brain-based therapy that taps directly into how your nervous system stores trauma. The body doesn’t lie. The mind does. Constantly. And EMDR cuts through that noise.

What Is EMDR Therapy? The Basics Without the Fluff

Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing, or EMDR, was developed by Francine Shapiro in the late 1980s. At first, it seemed odd. Shapiro noticed that moving her eyes back and forth reduced the intensity of disturbing thoughts. She then tested this with trauma survivors and found that bilateral stimulation—usually side-to-side eye movements—helped people reprocess traumatic memories without becoming overwhelmed.

Here’s what actually works: EMDR combines focused attention on distressing memories with rhythmic, bilateral stimulation. That stimulation can be eye movements, taps, or sounds. The goal isn’t to erase memories but to change how they’re stored in your brain. Instead of being “stuck” in a traumatic loop, these memories become less triggering and emotionally charged.

Neuroscience backs this up. The bilateral stimulation seems to engage both hemispheres of the brain, promoting integration and processing. According to Bessel van der Kolk, trauma gets “stuck” because the brain’s normal processing pathways are blocked. EMDR helps bypass those blocks.

Research on EMDR: What the Science Says

EMDR isn’t a new-age fad. It’s one of the few trauma therapies rigorously studied and endorsed by major organizations like the American Psychological Association and the World Health Organization. Studies consistently show it reduces symptoms of post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) faster than traditional talk therapy.

A 2018 meta-analysis published in the Journal of Anxiety Disorders reviewed 26 randomized controlled trials and found EMDR significantly reduced PTSD symptoms compared to control groups. It also showed effectiveness for related issues like anxiety and depression.

But here’s the catch: EMDR is not magic. It doesn’t work like flipping a switch. It requires a trained therapist to guide you safely through distressing memories. Over the years I’ve seen clients make progress faster with EMDR than with traditional talk therapy alone, especially when their trauma was deeply embedded in their body and nervous system.

Who Benefits From EMDR? Spoiler: It’s Not Just PTSD

Look, here’s the thing. Most people think EMDR is just for war veterans or sexual assault survivors. Well, yes, it’s effective for them, but it also helps with:

  • Complex trauma from childhood abuse or neglect
  • Phobias and specific fears
  • Grief and loss
  • Performance anxiety and panic attacks
  • Some cases of chronic pain and somatic symptoms

Stephen Porges’ work on the polyvagal theory explains why EMDR can be useful beyond PTSD. Trauma isn’t just psychological; it’s lodged in your nervous system’s regulation. Bilateral stimulation in EMDR can help shift your nervous system from stuck defensive states to more regulated ones.

That’s why people with complex trauma often find EMDR useful when combined with somatic therapies such as those developed by Pat Ogden or Peter Levine. While Levine’s Waking the Tiger focuses on body sensations and release, EMDR directly targets how memories get stored. The two can complement each other.

How Does EMDR Work Physically? Less Theory. More Practice.

This isn’t mystical. It’s mechanical. When you recall a traumatic memory, your brain’s limbic system lights up—especially the amygdala, the brain’s alarm bell. But your prefrontal cortex, responsible for rational thinking, often shuts down or disengages during trauma recall.

EMDR’s bilateral stimulation seems to activate both hemispheres, helping re-engage the prefrontal cortex and calm the amygdala’s alarm. In essence, it allows your brain to process the memory as safe and in the past, not as an ongoing threat.

Babette Rothschild describes how trauma therapy isn’t just about memory but about retraining the nervous system. EMDR facilitates this by providing a rhythmic stimulus that helps your brain reorganize the memory. It’s like the brain’s own “software update.”

The process usually involves eight phases: history taking, preparation, assessment, desensitization, installation, body scan, closure, and reevaluation. Your therapist will guide you through each, ensuring you’re grounded and safe as you process.

Getting Started With EMDR: What You Need to Know

In my own practice, I’ve seen people hesitate because they’re unsure if EMDR will be “too intense.” Here’s the thing: a well-trained therapist will tailor the pace to you. You’re never pushed beyond your window of tolerance. If you’re looking for resources, start with The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk. It’s a classic for understanding trauma’s effects on the brain and body.

EMDR isn’t for everyone. If you have certain medical conditions like seizures or severe dissociation, you’ll want to discuss risks with a professional. But if you’ve been stuck in trauma symptoms for years, it’s worth exploring.

Here’s what actually works: find a licensed therapist trained in EMDR, and commit to the process. It’s often faster than other therapies but requires your active participation and trust. The good news? Many people start feeling relief after just a few sessions.

For a deeper dive into nervous system regulation alongside EMDR, check out The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy by Deb Dana. It adds context for why the technique helps you feel safe in your body again.

Is EMDR only for eye movements?

No. While eye movements are the most common form of bilateral stimulation, therapists can also use taps or auditory tones. The key is rhythmic, alternating stimulation that helps engage both hemispheres of the brain.

How long does EMDR therapy usually take?

It depends on the individual and the trauma's complexity. Some people see improvement in as few as 6 to 12 sessions. Others with complex trauma may require more time. Progress tends to be faster than traditional talk therapy for PTSD.

Can I do EMDR on my own?

Stop overthinking this. EMDR should always be done with a trained therapist. Self-administering bilateral stimulation without guidance can be overwhelming and even retraumatizing. Safety and pacing are critical for effective healing.

EMDR is not some secret magic trick. It’s a practical, brain-based therapy that helps you process trauma faster and more thoroughly. If you’ve been stuck in old pain, it might be the tool you need. The body doesn’t lie. The mind does. Constantly. EMDR helps both tell the truth.

Reviewed by The Oracle Lover, Intuitive Educator and Oracle Guide. Last updated 2026-05-05.

title: Fight, Flight, Freeze, Fawn: Understanding Your Trauma Response
meta_description: Discover how fight, flight, freeze, and fawn responses affect trauma healing and learn somatic techniques to regain control and safety.
og_title: Fight, Flight, Freeze, Fawn: Understanding Your Trauma Response
og_description: Discover how fight, flight, freeze, and fawn responses affect trauma healing and learn somatic techniques to regain control and safety.
body:
Your trauma response is wired into your body as fight, flight, freeze, or fawn. These reactions are survival tools, not character flaws. Understanding them helps you reclaim control and heal.

Have you ever wondered why sometimes your body just freezes up in a stressful moment, or why you suddenly feel like running or fighting when nothing terribly dangerous is happening? Maybe you find yourself people-pleasing to avoid conflict, but you hate how that leaves you drained. What if I told you these reactions are your body’s oldest survival hacks, not your personal shortcomings? Fight, flight, freeze, and fawn are hardwired responses to trauma. They’re mechanical, not mystical. Understanding them can change how you relate to your body and your life.

Fight, Flight, Freeze, and Fawn: The Four Faces of Survival

Stop overthinking this. Your nervous system is designed to keep you alive. When it detects danger, it kicks into gear. You either fight the threat, run away (flight), shut down (freeze), or try to appease the threat (fawn). These are not just random behaviors. They’re deeply rooted in your biology.

Bruce Perry, a leading trauma researcher, highlights that these responses emerge from the brain’s hierarchical structure, especially the brainstem and limbic system, which operate below conscious control. These systems activate before your thinking brain even realizes it. This means your reactions often happen faster than your mind can catch up, explaining why you sometimes feel "out of control."

Understanding your default trauma response helps you recognize when you’re triggered. It also explains why traditional talk therapy alone often misses the mark for trauma survivors—because trauma lives in the body first, not just the mind.

Fight and Flight: When Your Body’s Alarm Goes Off Loud

Here’s what actually works: When you’re in fight mode, your body floods with adrenaline and cortisol. Your heart races, muscles tense, and your senses sharpen. You might yell, argue, or stand your ground aggressively. Flight is similar but geared toward escape. Your body prepares to bolt—heart pounding, breathing fast, legs ready to move.

Peter Levine, author of Waking the Tiger: Healing Trauma (paid link), explains that these responses are natural and necessary. The problem arises when you get stuck in them or when your nervous system gets triggered by non-life-threatening events—your boss’s email, a tense family dinner, or traffic.

In my own practice, I’ve seen clients confuse their fight or flight tendencies with personal flaws like anger issues or cowardice. They’re not flaws. They’re survival mechanisms. The trick is learning how to calm these responses down rather than trying to suppress or ignore them.

Freeze and Fawn: The Silent, Often Overlooked Responses

Freeze is less talked about but just as important. Bessel van der Kolk, in The Body Keeps the Score (paid link), describes freeze as the body’s last-ditch effort to survive when fight or flight isn’t possible. Your system shuts down, heart rate drops, and you feel numb or disconnected. It’s the body’s way of playing dead.

Fawn is the least understood response. Resmaa Menakem, a therapist focusing on racialized trauma, calls this the “people-pleaser” response in My Grandmother’s Hands (paid link). When you can’t fight, run, or freeze, you might try to appease or “fawn” to reduce threat. This looks like agreeing with others even when it hurts, over-accommodating, or losing your boundaries.

The body doesn’t lie. The mind does. Constantly. These responses aren’t your choice. They’re automatic. But awareness is the first step to change.

The Nervous System: Your Body’s Trauma Radar

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory revolutionized how we think about trauma. He identified that the vagus nerve controls many of these trauma responses through two branches: the ventral vagal and dorsal vagal systems. The ventral vagal supports social engagement and calm. The dorsal vagal triggers shutdown or freeze.

When you’re calm and connected, your ventral vagal system is active. But trauma flips the switch to dorsal vagal or sympathetic dominance, leading to freeze or fight/flight. This isn’t mystical. It’s mechanical. Your nervous system is constantly scanning for safety or danger. When it feels unsafe, it activates survival responses.

Babette Rothschild, in In an Unspoken Voice (paid link), points out that trauma treatment should focus on nervous system regulation first. Less theory. More practice. Mindfulness, breath work, and gentle movement can help shift your nervous system back to safety.

How to Work With Your Trauma Responses

Across the articles we’ve published on this site, one thing is clear: there is no magic pill for trauma. Healing is about small, consistent steps that teach your nervous system what safety feels like. Here’s what actually works:

  • Recognize your pattern: Are you fight, flight, freeze, or fawn? Notice your default response without judgment.
  • Get into your body: Trauma lives in the body. Somatic practices like body scans, grounding exercises, or slow movement help reconnect you.
  • Regulate your nervous system: Simple breath techniques or paced movement can calm your fight or flight. Engage social connection to activate ventral vagal.
  • Set boundaries: If fawning is your survival move, learning to say no and hold space for yourself is critical.
  • Seek support: Trauma-informed therapists trained in somatic approaches can guide you safely.

For more on nervous system regulation, check out The National Institute of Mental Health’s PTSD page. It explains how trauma reshapes your brain and body in scientific terms.

Can someone switch between different trauma responses?

Absolutely. Your trauma response isn’t fixed. It can change based on situation, context, or state of your nervous system. You might freeze when overwhelmed but fight when cornered. Recognizing your patterns helps you respond more consciously.

Is fawning always unhealthy?

Not necessarily. Sometimes fawning helps you navigate unsafe environments temporarily. The issue arises when fawning becomes your default, leading to loss of boundaries and chronic stress. Healing involves reclaiming your autonomy without fear.

How long does it take to heal these trauma responses?

There is no set timeline. Healing depends on many factors like severity of trauma, support systems, and your willingness to engage with your body. The key is patience and consistent practice, not rushing or forcing change.

Look, here’s the thing. Trauma responses are your body’s way of keeping you alive. They’re not your enemy. But if you want to live fully, you need to understand and work with them. That means paying attention to your body, learning to regulate your nervous system, and seeking support when needed. Healing is messy and nonlinear, but it’s possible.

Don’t let your trauma responses run the show. Take a step today to observe your reactions without judgment. Try a grounding exercise, a simple breath, or reach out to a trauma-informed professional. Your body remembers what happened, but it also remembers how to feel safe.

Reviewed by The Oracle Lover, Intuitive Educator and Oracle Guide. Last updated 2026-05-05.

title: Dissociation and Trauma: What's Happening in the Body
meta_description: Explore how trauma impacts the body through dissociation and discover effective somatic healing techniques to restore connection and wellbeing.
og_title: Dissociation and Trauma: What's Happening in the Body
og_description: Explore how trauma impacts the body through dissociation and discover effective somatic healing techniques to restore connection and wellbeing.
body:
Dissociation is the brain and body’s survival hack during trauma. It creates a split between experience and awareness to protect you from overwhelm. Healing means reconnecting your body and mind without retraumatizing yourself.

Ever felt like you’re somewhere else during a crisis, like your body is present but your mind has checked out? That’s dissociation. It’s not just “spacing out” or being flaky—it’s your nervous system’s raw, mechanical response to trauma. Look, here’s the thing. When trauma hits, your body is trying to keep you alive. Sometimes that means disconnecting from the moment. What’s really happening in your body during dissociation? And more importantly, how do you come back?

What Is Dissociation, Really?

Dissociation is a survival mechanism. When the brain perceives overwhelming threat, it can’t just fight or flee. Sometimes freezing isn’t enough. Dissociation is the nervous system’s way of protecting you by shutting down emotional and physical sensations. You might feel numb, detached, or as if you’re watching yourself from outside your body. This isn’t mystical. It’s mechanical. The body doesn’t lie. The mind does. Constantly.

Dr. Bessel van der Kolk, author of The Body Keeps the Score, describes dissociation as the brain’s attempt to block the flood of unbearable sensations. It’s a way to survive moments when your system is pushed beyond capacity. Your brain literally switches off parts of the experience, leaving you disconnected from your body and feelings.

The Body’s Role in Dissociation

Dissociation isn’t just a brain event. The body and nervous system play starring roles. Stephen Porges’s Polyvagal Theory explains how the autonomic nervous system (ANS) reacts to threat. When the sympathetic system (fight or flight) is overwhelmed, the parasympathetic system can trigger a shutdown, often called “freeze” or “collapse.” This shutdown is linked to dissociation.

In this state, your heart rate slows, your muscles loosen, and your awareness dims. It’s like your system is hitting the reset button to protect you from injury or death. But that reset can get stuck. The trauma isn’t processed, and your nervous system learns to dissociate as a default response to stress.

Pat Ogden, a pioneer in somatic experiencing, highlights how trauma locks the body into patterns of tension or collapse. The body remembers what the mind forgets. In other words, trauma isn’t just “in your head.” It’s in the way your muscles hold tension or your nervous system freezes. This is why trauma work needs to meet the body where it is, not just the cognitive story.

How Dissociation Shows Up in Daily Life

Dissociation can feel subtle or extreme. You might zone out during conversations, forget chunks of time, or feel emotionally numb. Some people describe it as a feeling of emptiness or detachment from reality. These are your nervous system’s echoes of past traumas.

Resmaa Menakem, author of My Grandmother’s Hands, points out that trauma and dissociation often manifest in the body’s “holding patterns.” Chronic tension in the shoulders or jaw, shallow breathing, or a sense of being “disconnected” can all signal an underlying dissociative response.

Across the articles we've published on this site, I’ve seen countless people surprised when they realize their “spacing out” moments are not laziness or distraction — they’re safety strategies gone into overdrive.

Why You Can’t Just “Snap Out of It”

Stop overthinking this: dissociation is not a choice. You don’t “check out” because you want to. Your nervous system is reacting to protect you. Forcing yourself to “wake up” or “face it” without proper support can retraumatize you. Bruce Perry, a leading trauma researcher, emphasizes the importance of safety and pacing in trauma recovery. The brain needs to feel safe before it can reconnect with dissociated parts of experience.

Babette Rothschild, in her work on trauma and the body, says that pushing too hard too fast can overwhelm the nervous system, causing more shutdowns or flashbacks. Less theory. More practice. Healing dissociation means helping the body slowly learn it’s safe to feel again without flooding the system.

Here’s What Actually Works: Somatic Approaches to Healing Dissociation

Healing dissociation is about restoring connection between body, brain, and mind. Somatic therapies focus on what’s happening physically—breathing, movement, posture, and sensation—to gently bring you back into your body. Peter Levine’s Waking the Tiger explains how trauma is held in the body’s nervous system and how titrated exposure to sensation can help release it.

One practical step you can take today is called "pendulation." It’s the nervous system’s natural rhythm between states of activation and calm. When you feel dissociated, try noticing a small sensation in your body—a finger twitch, a breath moving in your belly. Then slowly shift your attention to a neutral or positive sensation, like the feeling of your feet on the floor. Back and forth. This teaches your nervous system it can tolerate sensation without shutting down.

Dan Siegel, known for his work on interpersonal neurobiology, highlights the importance of integration. He says the goal is to link the disconnected parts of your brain with your body awareness. Mindfulness, safe movement, and grounding exercises help build this integration.

If you want to dive deeper, this NIH article offers a solid overview of dissociation and trauma from a neurobiological perspective.

For practical tools, consider books like Anchored: How to Befriend Your Nervous System which offers accessible techniques for managing nervous system states.

Is dissociation the same as daydreaming or zoning out?

No. While daydreaming or zoning out are common and usually harmless, dissociation is a deeper response tied to trauma and overwhelm. It often involves a feeling of disconnection from your body or reality and can interfere with daily functioning.

Can dissociation be treated without therapy?

It depends on the severity. Mild dissociation can sometimes be managed with self-regulation techniques, mindfulness, and grounding. However, persistent or severe dissociation usually requires professional support to safely process underlying trauma.

How long does it take to heal dissociation?

Healing is a gradual process that varies for each person. Reconnecting with your body and nervous system without retraumatizing yourself takes time and patience. Some people find relief within months, while others work on it for years. Consistent practice and support are key.

Healing from dissociation means reclaiming your body and your experience. It means learning to be present with yourself, even when it’s hard. Stop overthinking this. The body is waiting to come back online. Your nervous system wants to feel safe. Your mind isn’t broken, it’s just confused. The path back is in your breath, your movement, and your attention.

So here’s my challenge to you: next time you notice yourself drifting away or feeling numb, don’t judge it. Instead, try a small grounding step. Feel your feet on the floor. Notice your breath. This isn’t therapy, it’s biology. The body doesn’t lie. The mind does. Constantly.

Reviewed by The Oracle Lover, Intuitive Educator and Oracle Guide. Last updated 2026-05-05.

title: TRE: Tension and Trauma Releasing Exercises Explained
meta_description: Discover how TRE Tension and Trauma Releasing Exercises help release deep muscle tension to support somatic trauma healing and emotional recovery.
og_title: TRE: Tension and Trauma Releasing Exercises Explained
og_description: Discover how TRE Tension and Trauma Releasing Exercises help release deep muscle tension to support somatic trauma healing and emotional recovery.
body:
Tension and Trauma Releasing Exercises (TRE) use your body’s natural shaking response to release stress and trauma. This isn’t mystical. It’s mechanical, rooted in neurobiology. Doing TRE regularly helps reset your nervous system and ease stored tension.

You know that feeling when stress gets stuck deep in your body — your neck tightens, your chest feels heavy, or your legs won’t stop trembling? What if I told you that trembling isn’t just a sign of being scared or cold, but actually a built-in healing mechanism? TRE, or Tension and Trauma Releasing Exercises, taps into this automatic shaking to help your body shake off trauma. It’s not about wishful thinking or vague energy work. It’s about activating your body’s biology to reset your nervous system. No woo-woo, no fluff.

What Is TRE and Why Does It Work?

TRE was developed by Dr. David Berceli after years of working with war survivors and disaster victims. He noticed that after traumatic events, people’s bodies would often tremble spontaneously, which seemed to help release tension and calm them down. He created a series of simple exercises that intentionally trigger this tremoring response. Think of it as helping your body do what it already knows how to do — shake off stress.

Here’s what actually works: Your nervous system has a built-in reflex to restore balance after threat. According to Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory, your vagus nerve helps regulate your “rest and digest” state. When trauma or stress overwhelms you, your body gets stuck in a “fight, flight, or freeze” response. TRE activates the tremors that signal the nervous system to de-escalate. It’s like hitting the reset button.

Over the years I’ve seen clients shift from chronic anxiety and muscle tension to calmer, more grounded states simply by doing these exercises consistently. You don’t have to overthink this. The body doesn’t lie. The mind does. Constantly.

The Science Behind the Shakes

Peter Levine, author of Waking the Tiger: Healing Trauma, has long championed the idea that trauma is stored in the body. His Somatic Experiencing approach shares a concept with TRE: allowing the body to complete the natural defense response through movement or shaking.

Research, although still emerging, supports that tremoring helps reduce hyperarousal in the nervous system. A study published in the Journal of Bodywork and Movement Therapies found that participants who engaged in TRE showed reduced muscle tension and improved emotional regulation. This isn’t magic. It’s physiology. Your muscles contract under stress, and shaking them out stimulates the parasympathetic nervous system, which calms the body down.

Bessel van der Kolk, in his book The Body Keeps the Score, highlights how trauma disrupts the body's ability to regulate itself. TRE offers a way to regain some of that regulation by engaging the body directly, bypassing the often overactive or overwhelmed mind.

How TRE Exercises Are Done

TRE is deceptively simple. You start with a set of gentle stretches and movements designed to fatigue certain muscle groups, especially in the legs and hips. That fatigue triggers your body’s shaking response. The tremors themselves are involuntary, and you’re encouraged to just let them happen without trying to control or stop them.

The exercises can be done lying down or standing, making them accessible for most people. Each session lasts about 10 to 20 minutes, though you can repeat the tremoring part several times during a session. The goal is not to force anything, but to create conditions where your body naturally shifts out of tension.

Babette Rothschild, a trauma therapist and author, stresses the importance of safety and pacing in somatic practices like TRE. If the shaking feels overwhelming, you should stop and come back to it later. This is less about pushing through and more about listening to your body’s signals.

Who Can Benefit and Who Should Be Careful?

Look, here’s the thing. TRE is not a cure-all for every kind of trauma or tension, but it’s a valuable tool in your self-care toolkit. People with chronic stress, mild to moderate trauma, or anyone who feels stuck in their body can benefit. It’s also used by athletes, first responders, and others who experience high levels of physical or emotional stress.

That said, if you have severe trauma, complex PTSD, or certain medical conditions (like epilepsy or serious heart problems), you should consult a healthcare professional before starting TRE. Experienced TRE providers can help tailor the exercises and guide you safely.

Resmaa Menakem, author of My Grandmother's Hands, reminds us that trauma is also racialized and stored in body memory differently across communities. TRE’s accessibility can help, but it’s no replacement for broader systemic healing efforts.

Practical Tips for Getting Started

Stop overthinking this. If you want to try TRE, start slow. Find a quiet space, wear comfortable clothes, and follow a guided video or class if possible. You can find certified TRE providers online or try apps and DVDs. Consistency beats intensity.

In my own practice, clients often report better sleep, reduced muscle pain, and increased emotional stability after just a few weeks of regular TRE. Here’s what actually works: commit to 10 minutes a day. Notice how your body feels before and after. Keep a journal if you want.

Want some reading to back up your practice? Check out The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy by Deb Dana for a deeper understanding of the nervous system, or Anchored: How to Befriend Your Nervous System by Deb Dana for tools to calm your body and mind.

Is TRE safe for everyone?

Generally, TRE is safe for most people, but it’s not for everyone. If you have severe trauma, cardiovascular issues, or neurological conditions, talk to a healthcare provider first. Start slowly and pay attention to your body’s signals. It’s better to work with a certified TRE practitioner if you have complex trauma.

How often should I do TRE?

Consistency matters more than frequency. Starting with 10-20 minutes, 3 to 5 times a week is a good rule of thumb. You can do it daily if it feels good and you don’t get overwhelmed. The goal is to gradually calm your nervous system without triggering intense emotional reactions.

Can TRE replace therapy?

TRE is a helpful tool but not a replacement for therapy, especially if you have deep trauma or mental health conditions. It’s best used alongside professional treatment or as part of a broader self-care routine. If you’re working with a therapist, mention TRE so they can help integrate it safely.

Trauma changes how your nervous system behaves, but it doesn’t have to define your life. TRE offers a simple, body-based way to reclaim calm and ease. The body remembers what the mind forgets. Start small, trust the process, and give your nervous system a much-needed break. You deserve that.

Reviewed by The Oracle Lover, Intuitive Educator and Oracle Guide. Last updated 2026-05-05.

title: The Vagus Nerve and Trauma: What You Actually Need to Know
meta_description: Discover how the vagus nerve impacts trauma and learn effective somatic healing techniques to support recovery and emotional regulation.
og_title: The Vagus Nerve and Trauma: What You Actually Need to Know
og_description: Discover how the vagus nerve impacts trauma and learn effective somatic healing techniques to support recovery and emotional regulation.
body:
The vagus nerve is central to how trauma affects your body and mind. It controls your nervous system’s balance, not just your "relaxation response." Trauma disrupts it in ways you can learn to recognize and gently heal.

Look, here’s the thing. The vagus nerve has become the poster child for trauma healing, hyped as a miracle switch you can flip to cure all your anxiety, stress, and emotional pain. But what if I told you it’s not magic? It’s not some mystical energy you just “activate” with a few breaths or a fancy device. It’s a complex, biological system that gets overwhelmed, damaged, and stuck—just like you do—when trauma hits. Understanding how it works is not about chasing buzzwords, it’s about knowing what’s actually happening inside your body so you can stop spinning your wheels and start healing.

The Vagus Nerve: Your Body’s Communication Highway

The vagus nerve is the longest cranial nerve in your body. It runs from your brainstem all the way down to your gut, connecting your heart, lungs, digestive tract, and more. Stephen Porges, who developed the Polyvagal Theory, showed us that the vagus is not a single line of communication but a dual system. There’s the myelinated “ventral vagal” pathway, which helps you stay calm, socially engaged, and connected to the world. Then there’s the unmyelinated “dorsal vagal” pathway, which can shut things down, slow your heart, and trigger freeze or collapse responses.

This isn’t mystical. It’s mechanical. When trauma overwhelms your nervous system, it tends to push you away from that ventral vagal state into defensive modes: fight, flight, or freeze. Your body isn’t failing you—it’s doing exactly what it’s designed to do, but the problem is that this response can get stuck. Chronic trauma keeps you locked out of your natural calm and safety responses, leaving you dysregulated and disconnected.

Trauma’s Impact on the Vagus Nerve and Nervous System

Bessel van der Kolk, author of The Body Keeps the Score (paid link), has emphasized how trauma literally changes your body’s wiring. It’s not just a psychological thing. The vagus nerve’s ability to regulate your heart rate, digestion, and breathing is impaired by trauma. This creates a feedback loop where your body remains in high alert or shutdown mode, which feeds anxiety, depression, and physical symptoms like digestive issues or chronic pain.

Pat Ogden, a pioneer in somatic psychology, points out that trauma survivors often lose “interoception,” the ability to sense what’s happening inside their own bodies. This loss is tied directly to vagal dysfunction. If you can’t feel your heartbeat or your breathing in a grounded way, you can’t use your body’s natural cues to calm down. This is why trauma healing can’t be just talk therapy. You need to get back into your body, gently reactivating the ventral vagal pathway through movement, breath, and safe social connection.

Practical Ways to Support Your Vagus Nerve

Stop overthinking this. The vagus nerve responds best to simple, consistent practice. Dan Siegel, a clinical professor of psychiatry, highlights the importance of “window of tolerance” work. This means learning to stay within a zone where your nervous system feels safe enough to process emotions without going into fight, flight, or freeze. You do that by tuning into your body and using exercises that gently stimulate your ventral vagal system.

  • Slow, deep breathing with extended exhales activates the ventral vagal nerve.
  • Gentle cold exposure like splashing cold water on your face can help reset your nervous system.
  • Vocal exercises like humming, chanting, or singing stimulate the vocal cords and improve vagal tone.
  • Safe social connection—eye contact, laughter, and touch—engages the social engagement system tied to the ventral vagal nerve.

Babette Rothschild reminds us in In an Unspoken Voice (paid link) that trauma healing happens in the body first. It’s about learning to tolerate sensations without dissociating or shutting down. Less theory. More practice. That means you don’t need to understand every detail about the vagus nerve to heal. You just need to consistently do what calms your body and mind.

Why The Vagus Nerve is Not a Quick Fix

Over the years I’ve seen countless people get frustrated because they tried “vagus nerve hacks”—like cold showers, deep breathing apps, or vagus nerve stimulators—and felt no change. Here’s why: trauma rewires your nervous system over time. It’s not just about activating the nerve on demand. It’s about repairing your nervous system’s overall capacity to regulate itself.

Bruce Perry, a child psychiatrist who studies trauma, teaches us that it takes “bottom-up” work to heal trauma. That means starting with your body and nervous system before your mind. You can’t just think your way out of trauma. The vagus nerve is part of a bigger system that needs gentle, ongoing care. That includes predictable routines, safety, and connection.

Resmaa Menakem, in his book My Grandmother’s Hands (paid link), emphasizes the importance of understanding trauma’s racialized and cultural contexts. Stress on the vagus nerve is not just individual; it’s collective. Healing requires addressing social and systemic factors too. This means your trauma healing journey is about more than just nerves. It’s about reclaiming your body in a world that often doesn’t feel safe.

Where to Go From Here

Look, here’s the thing. If you’re overwhelmed by vagus nerve hype, relax. The body doesn’t lie. The mind does. Constantly. Your nervous system is honest. It’s telling you what it needs through your sensations, emotions, and behaviors. Your job is to listen, not overthink, and respond with kindness and curiosity.

Start small. Build basic safety. Practice simple breathwork or social engagement. Notice how your body reacts. If you want to read more, start with Porges’ The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link). But don’t get stuck in theory—it’s about practice. Your vagus nerve is not a switch. It’s a system that needs time, patience, and consistency.

If you want the science, here’s an authoritative review on vagus nerve stimulation and trauma from the National Institutes of Health. It’s technical but shows the current evidence clearly.

Can I heal vagus nerve dysfunction on my own?

Yes, but carefully. Many simple exercises like paced breathing, humming, and cold exposure can improve vagal tone. Still, if you have severe trauma or PTSD symptoms, working with a trauma-informed therapist ensures you stay safe and supported.

Is vagus nerve stimulation a reliable trauma treatment?

Vagus nerve stimulation (VNS) devices show promise in research but are not yet a mainstream trauma cure. They may help regulate the nervous system, but trauma healing also requires emotional processing and social connection.

Why does trauma affect digestion and heart rate?

The vagus nerve controls your parasympathetic nervous system, which manages digestion and heart rate. Trauma dysregulates the vagus, causing gastrointestinal issues and heart rate variability problems often seen in trauma survivors.

Trauma isn’t just stuck in your mind. It’s stored deep in your nervous system, especially in the vagus nerve’s pathways. Understanding what’s going on biologically breaks the spell of confusion and hopelessness around trauma healing. Your body is a source of truth, and your vagus nerve is one of its main messengers. Take care of it with patience and respect. The rest will follow.

Reviewed by The Oracle Lover, Intuitive Educator and Oracle Guide. Last updated 2026-05-05.

title: Sensorimotor Psychotherapy: Pat Ogden's Body-First Approach
meta_description: Discover how Sensorimotor Psychotherapy uses body-centered techniques to heal somatic trauma through Pat Ogden's innovative approach.
og_title: Sensorimotor Psychotherapy: Pat Ogden's Body-First Approach
og_description: Discover how Sensorimotor Psychotherapy uses body-centered techniques to heal somatic trauma through Pat Ogden's innovative approach.
body:
Sensorimotor Psychotherapy treats trauma through the body first. It’s about what you feel, not just what you think. Healing happens when you engage your nervous system, not just your mind.

Have you ever tried to talk through a trauma and felt like you were hitting a wall? Like your words just bounced off something inside you? That’s because trauma isn’t just stored in your brain. It’s stuck in your body. Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, developed by Pat Ogden, takes this reality seriously. It says: before you can heal the mind, you’ve got to work with the body. No fluff, no psycho-babble. Just real, practical steps to get your nervous system back on track.

Why The Body Comes First

Look, here’s the thing. The body doesn’t lie. The mind does. Constantly. Bessel van der Kolk, author of The Body Keeps the Score, has shown us that trauma isn’t just memories or thoughts. It’s a physical wound. When something traumatic happens, your brain’s alarm system kicks in and your body goes into survival mode. That might mean freezing, fleeing, or fighting, even if you’re safe now. The problem? Your body still remembers. That stuck energy can cause anxiety, pain, dissociation, or a feeling of numbness.

Pat Ogden took this insight and ran with it. She combined talk therapy with movement, posture, and breath—all the stuff your brain can’t always reach. It’s about learning to recognize what your body is telling you, then helping it shift out of trauma patterns. This isn’t mystical. It’s mechanical. Your nervous system responds to sensations, not thoughts. So, you need an approach that works with your sensations first.

The Role Of The Nervous System In Trauma Healing

Stephen Porges, the father of Polyvagal Theory, helped us understand trauma through the lens of the nervous system. He explained how the vagus nerve controls our ability to feel safe or threatened. When trauma happens, your system often gets stuck in defensive modes. Sensorimotor Psychotherapy pays close attention to these states, teaching you how to sense when you’re in fight, flight, or freeze—and, more importantly, how to shift out of them.

In my own practice, I’ve seen how just noticing small shifts in posture, or the way someone breathes, can open the door to real change. This is why Sensorimotor Psychotherapy doesn’t just focus on what you say in therapy. It trains you to tune into your body’s signals—like tension in your shoulders or shallow breathing—and work with them.

How Sensorimotor Psychotherapy Works

Less theory. More practice. Sensorimotor Psychotherapy is hands-on. It’s about:

  • Tracking physical sensations.
  • Exploring posture and movement habits.
  • Using gentle, guided movement to shift stuck patterns.
  • Building a new sense of safety within your body.

For example, if you find your shoulders are always hunched after trauma, your therapist might help you notice this and try small movements to release tension. You’ll learn how to calm your nervous system by changing your breathing or shifting your gaze. The goal is to retrain your body to be in the present, not locked in the past.

Babette Rothschild, another big name in trauma therapy, emphasizes that trauma lives in the body and that helping clients regain control over their physiological responses is critical. Sensorimotor sits well with this idea. You don’t just talk about trauma—you change your body’s reaction to it.

Where Sensorimotor Fits With Other Modalities

Peter Levine’s Somatic Experiencing also works with the body, focusing on releasing trauma through bodily sensations. Sensorimotor Psychotherapy adds a structured way to link these sensations to your thoughts and emotions. It bridges the gap between body and mind without making it a guessing game.

Resmaa Menakem’s work on racial trauma highlights how trauma impacts the body differently depending on experience and identity. Sensorimotor’s attentiveness to the body means it can adapt to these differences by focusing on each person’s unique physical responses.

Dan Siegel talks about integration—connecting body, mind, and relationships. Sensorimotor Psychotherapy helps you build that internal integration by first calming your nervous system, so you’re not stuck in reactive survival mode. Once your body feels safer, your mind can get clearer. That's when real progress happens.

Where To Start If You Want To Try This

Stop overthinking this. Healing trauma with your body starts with simple awareness. Notice your posture right now. How’s your breathing? Are you tense anywhere? That’s your starting point. Sensorimotor Psychotherapy teaches you to track these signals and work with them in therapy or even on your own.

To get a deeper understanding, I recommend these books: Waking the Tiger: Healing Trauma by Peter Levine, which lays out the physical nature of trauma; The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy by Stephen Porges, which explains nervous system states; and Anchored: How to Befriend Your Nervous System, a practical guide to calming your body.

Also, check out the National Institutes of Health article on Sensorimotor Psychotherapy for some peer-reviewed insights on its effectiveness.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is Sensorimotor Psychotherapy only for severe trauma?

No. While it’s very effective for severe trauma, it also helps with everyday stress, anxiety, and somatic complaints. It’s about learning to listen to your body, which anyone can benefit from.

Can I practice Sensorimotor techniques on my own?

Yes, many techniques like breath awareness, gentle movement, and body scanning can be done solo. However, working with a trained therapist helps ensure you’re safe and guided through deeper trauma layers.

How long does healing with Sensorimotor Psychotherapy take?

There’s no set timeline. Healing depends on your trauma’s complexity, your nervous system, and how much you practice. Some people notice changes quickly; for others, it’s a gradual process.

Wrapping It Up

If you’ve ever felt stuck in your healing, like your mind is willing but your body won’t cooperate, Sensorimotor Psychotherapy offers a clear, body-first path forward. The body holds the key—and the lock—to trauma recovery. You’re not broken. Your system just needs to be guided back to balance. So start small. Notice. Move. Breathe. This isn’t mystical. It’s mechanical. And here’s what actually works.
