title: Somatic Therapy vs Talk Therapy: When the Body Needs More Than Words
meta_description: Discover how somatic therapy heals trauma through the body and when it offers more effective relief than traditional talk therapy.
og_title: Somatic Therapy vs Talk Therapy: When the Body Needs More Than Words
og_description: Discover how somatic therapy heals trauma through the body and when it offers more effective relief than traditional talk therapy.
body:
Somatic therapy works with the body, not just the mind. Talk therapy alone can miss trauma stored physically. Healing requires more than words—it needs felt experience.

Look, here’s the thing. If you think trauma is only in your head, you’re missing half the picture. You can spend years talking about what happened, but if your body still reacts like you’re in danger, words won’t cut it. The body doesn’t lie. The mind does. Constantly. So what happens when talking it out isn’t enough? That’s when somatic therapy steps in.

Why Talk Therapy Isn’t Always Enough

Talk therapy has been the go-to for mental health for decades. It’s great for some problems—cognitive distortions, relationship issues, depression, anxiety. But trauma is different. It’s encoded not just as memories but as physical responses. When you relive a traumatic event just by hearing a sound or a smell, that’s your body reacting, not your conscious mind.

Bessel van der Kolk, a leading trauma researcher, explains this in The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). He highlights how trauma reshapes brain function and rewires the nervous system. Talk therapy can help you understand trauma, but it can’t always fix the underlying biological imprint. You might talk about feeling safe, but your body still tenses up in a crowded room or freezes at a loud noise.

In my own practice, I’ve seen people endlessly stuck in cycles of retraumatization despite years of therapy. The missing piece? The body’s story wasn’t being heard. Trauma lives in muscles, breath, posture, and nervous system arousal. If you ignore that, you’re only treating half the problem.

What Somatic Therapy Actually Does

Somatic therapy is about tuning into your body to release trapped trauma. It’s less about telling your story and more about feeling your story. Peter Levine, author of Waking the Tiger: Healing Trauma (paid link), developed Somatic Experiencing based on the idea that trauma is a “stuck” fight, flight, or freeze response in the body.

Here’s what actually works: slow, gentle attention to bodily sensations combined with safe, controlled activation of the nervous system. When you start to feel the tension, the trembling, the breath irregularity, you can begin to discharge the trauma physically instead of just thinking about it. This often looks like grounding exercises, breath work, or movement combined with therapist guidance.

It’s not mystical. It’s mechanical. The nervous system can recalibrate, but it needs practice and patience. Stephen Porges’s Polyvagal Theory, explained in The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link), shows how our nervous system toggles between states of safety, danger, and shutdown. Somatic therapy helps you shift from a defensive freeze state into safety, by tuning into your body’s cues and creating new patterns.

When Should You Choose Somatic Therapy Over Talk Therapy?

Stop overthinking this: if you’re stuck, anxious, or feeling disconnected in your body, talk therapy may not be enough. Here are some signs you might need somatic therapy:

  • Your body reacts strongly to reminders of trauma (e.g., panic attacks, muscle tightness, dissociation).
  • You’ve gone through talk therapy but still feel “numb” or disconnected.
  • You have chronic pain, digestive issues, or fatigue with no clear medical cause.
  • You experience sudden emotional or physical shutdowns without clear triggers.

Pat Ogden, a pioneer in somatic psychology, emphasizes that trauma changes the body’s sensory-motor system, which is why traditional therapy often misses these symptoms. Somatic therapy engages this system directly, making it a better fit when words alone don’t help.

Across the articles we’ve published on this site, one thing’s clear: healing includes the body. Ignoring your body is like trying to fix a computer by only looking at the screen and never opening the case.

How Somatic Therapy Works: Practical Techniques

Less theory. More practice. Here’s what somatic therapy might look like in session:

  • Body scanning: You’re guided to notice areas of tension, warmth, or numbness.
  • Breath regulation: Learning to lengthen and deepen breath to calm the nervous system.
  • Movement and release: Small movements or shakes to discharge held tension.
  • Anchoring: Using touch or visualization to find feelings of safety in the body (see Anchored: How to Befriend Your Nervous System for more on this).

Babette Rothschild notes that these techniques help clients stay present without flooding or dissociating. It’s about learning to tolerate and regulate feelings rather than avoiding them.

For those interested in the science behind it, this NIH article on somatic therapies for trauma offers a thorough review of recent research showing positive outcomes in PTSD, anxiety, and depression.

Combining Talk and Somatic Therapies

You don’t have to choose one or the other. Often, the best results come from blending approaches. Dan Siegel’s work emphasizes the importance of integrating mind and body for healing. You might start with talk therapy to process your story, then add somatic techniques to calm your nervous system and release physical tension.

Trauma is complex, but don’t let complexity paralyze you. Whether you’re new to therapy or have years of experience, consider adding a somatic approach if you feel “stuck” or disconnected. The body keeps what the mind forgets.

Can somatic therapy replace talk therapy?

No. Somatic therapy complements talk therapy rather than replacing it. While somatic therapy focuses on body sensations and nervous system regulation, talk therapy helps with cognitive processing and emotional understanding. Together, they create a fuller healing experience.

Is somatic therapy only for severe trauma?

Not at all. Somatic therapy can help anyone who experiences stress or emotional overwhelm, including anxiety, depression, or physical symptoms linked to trauma. It’s about reconnecting with your body and nervous system, which benefits many different conditions.

How do I find a qualified somatic therapist?

Look for therapists trained in Somatic Experiencing, Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, or certified in Polyvagal Theory applications. Always check credentials and ask about their experience with trauma. Referrals from trusted sources or professional directories are a good place to start.

Healing isn’t just about talking. It’s about feeling safe in your body again. If you keep revisiting the trauma in your mind without relief, it’s time to listen to your body. Try some somatic work. Move, breathe, feel. The body remembers what the mind can’t always say.

Reviewed by The Oracle Lover, Intuitive Educator and Oracle Guide. Last updated 2026-05-05.

title: The Fawn Response: People-Pleasing as a Trauma Survival Strategy
meta_description: Explore how the fawn response drives people-pleasing as a trauma survival strategy and learn somatic healing techniques to reclaim your boundaries.
og_title: The Fawn Response: People-Pleasing as a Trauma Survival Strategy
og_description: Explore how the fawn response drives people-pleasing as a trauma survival strategy and learn somatic healing techniques to reclaim your boundaries.
body:
The fawn response is a survival strategy rooted in trauma. It shows up as chronic people-pleasing to avoid harm. Healing requires understanding your nervous system, not just your thoughts.

Have you ever caught yourself bending over backwards to keep the peace, even when it costs your sanity? Maybe you call it being kind or helpful, but deep down, it’s survival. The fawn response is trauma’s quiet handshake. It’s not weakness or codependency. It’s your nervous system saying, “Don’t make waves. Stay safe.”

What Is the Fawn Response? Trauma’s Subtle Survival Trick

When we talk about trauma responses, you probably think fight, flight, or freeze. Those are well-known. But there’s a fourth, less talked about: fawn. Introduced by therapist Pete Walker, this response is about appeasing—trying to please others to avoid conflict or danger.

Look, here’s the thing. The fawn response often flies under the radar because it looks like “nice” behavior. But it’s a learned survival strategy. If you grew up in an environment where anger or disapproval felt dangerous, your nervous system learned to soften, comply, charm, or sacrifice to stay out of trouble.

Bessel van der Kolk, in The Body Keeps the Score (paid link), emphasizes how trauma rewires your brain and body, creating these automatic responses. The fawn response is no exception: it’s your body’s way of protecting you from perceived threats by keeping the aggressor calm.

The Biology Behind People-Pleasing

The body doesn’t lie. The mind does. Constantly. When you engage in people-pleasing, your nervous system is often stuck in a state of hypervigilance or shutdown. Stephen Porges, who developed the Polyvagal Theory, explains that your nervous system has layers: the ventral vagal state is safe and social, but when threatened, your system shifts to fight, flight, or freeze.

The fawn response happens when neither fight nor flight feels safe or effective. Instead, you go into appeasement mode—calming the aggressor by being overly compliant. This is a survival trick from the autonomic nervous system, not a conscious choice. It’s mechanical, not mystical.

If you want to explore this further, Porges’s book The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link) offers a clear look at how your vagus nerve shapes your reactions to trauma and social cues.

How People-Pleasing Keeps Trauma Alive

In my own practice, I see a pattern: clients who constantly people-please often don’t realize they’re stuck in survival mode. It feels normal because it’s been their coping mechanism for so long.

Pat Ogden, founder of Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, points out that trauma is not just in your head but stored in your body’s implicit memory. When you fawn, parts of your body are still trying to keep you safe based on past trauma—even if the current situation doesn’t require it.

This creates a feedback loop. You please others to avoid conflict, but you sacrifice your needs and boundaries. That leads to resentment, burnout, and more internal distress. It’s not just emotional. It’s physiological stress keeping your nervous system activated.

Healing the Fawn Response: Less Theory. More Practice.

Stop overthinking this. Healing the fawn response is about retraining your nervous system to feel safe without needing to please everyone. You don’t just “think your way out” of this pattern.

Dan Siegel’s work on interpersonal neurobiology shows us how relationships and attuned presence can help regulate the nervous system. Safe connection is the antidote to trauma. The next time you feel the urge to people-please, pause and check in with your body. Where do you feel tension? What sensations come up? This awareness is your first step.

Bruce Perry emphasizes that healing trauma needs repeated, safe experiences—not just talk therapy. Grounding techniques, like focused breathing or gentle movement, can help your nervous system settle. A great resource for this is Anchored: How to Befriend Your Nervous System (paid link).

Here’s what actually works: set small boundaries and get comfortable with discomfort. Practice saying no in low-stakes situations. Notice your nervous system’s response and breathe through it. The more practice you get, the more your biology rewires confidence over compliance.

Recognizing the Roots: Early Attachment and the Fawn Response

Attachment trauma plays a huge role in the fawn response. Babette Rothschild highlights how early experiences with caregivers teach your nervous system what safety looks like. If love came with conditions or threats, your brain learned that pleasing was survival.

Over the years I’ve seen people confuse their worth with their ability to please others. That’s not your fault. It’s a defense mechanism that developed when you needed it. Understanding this can help you treat yourself with compassion rather than judgment.

Resmaa Menakem’s work in racialized trauma and somatic healing also shows how cultural and generational trauma can deepen these survival patterns. You’re not just healing you. You’re healing systems.

Is the fawn response always bad?

No. The fawn response is a survival strategy, so in dangerous or abusive situations, it can protect you from harm. Problems arise when it becomes automatic, chronic, and interferes with healthy relationships and self-care.

How can I tell if I’m stuck in the fawn response?

Look for patterns of excessive people-pleasing, difficulty saying no, low self-worth tied to approval, and ignoring your own needs to keep peace. Paying attention to your body’s feelings during these moments can help you spot it.

What’s a quick practice to begin healing the fawn response?

Start with noticing your urge to please. When it arises, take a breath and ask yourself, “What do I really need right now?” Try setting one small boundary each day, even if it feels uncomfortable. Over time, your nervous system will learn safety in asserting your needs.

Stop overthinking this. People-pleasing is not about personality flaws or lack of willpower. It’s biology. Trauma rewired your system for survival. Healing it means rewiring for safety and authenticity.

Think about it: how much of your energy goes toward keeping others comfortable at your expense? What if you could retrain your nervous system to feel safe with honesty and boundaries? That’s the challenge—and the invitation.

Start small. Practice presence in your body. Allow yourself to say no without guilt. Reach for resources and helpers who understand the body’s role in trauma—because this isn’t mystical. It’s mechanical.

Across the articles we’ve published on this site, the message is clear: understanding your body’s language is the key to healing trauma. The fawn response may feel like a cage, but it’s one you can unlock with patience and practice.

Reviewed by The Oracle Lover, Intuitive Educator and Oracle Guide. Last updated 2026-05-05.

title: Chronic Pain and Trauma: The Body's Unfinished Business
meta_description: Explore how chronic pain links to trauma and discover effective somatic healing techniques to release the body's trapped stress and restore well-being.
og_title: Chronic Pain and Trauma: The Body's Unfinished Business
og_description: Explore how chronic pain links to trauma and discover effective somatic healing techniques to release the body's trapped stress and restore well-being.
body:
Chronic pain often reflects unresolved trauma stored in your body. Trauma changes how your nervous system responds to stress, keeping pain alive. Healing means addressing the body’s unfinished business, not just your symptoms.

Imagine living with pain every day, no matter what you do. You’ve tried meds, therapy, maybe even surgery. Nothing sticks. Here’s the thing: your pain might not be just a physical issue. It’s a message from trauma that never got settled. The body doesn’t lie. The mind does. Constantly. If you want relief, you need to listen to what your body is still holding onto.

Chronic Pain Isn’t Just in Your Tissues

Most doctors focus on physical damage — inflammation, nerve injury, or wear and tear. But chronic pain often outlasts the original injury or shows up without any clear cause. That’s because your nervous system plays a major role in what you feel as pain. Bessel van der Kolk, a pioneer in trauma research, argues that trauma leaves deep imprints on your brain and body. These imprints don’t just vanish when the physical injury heals.

Think of trauma as “unfinished business” for your nervous system. It’s stuck in a heightened state of alarm, triggering pain signals long after the threat is gone. This state primes your body for chronic pain, fatigue, and even emotional distress. You can’t separate the mind from the body here. They’re locked in a loop.

In my own practice, I’ve seen people with no clear physical cause for their pain finally find relief when they address trauma patterns held in their bodies. That’s why The Body Keeps the Score by van der Kolk remains a must-read — it explains this connection clearly, without fluff.

How Trauma Rewires Your Nervous System

Stop overthinking this: trauma isn’t just an event from the past. It’s an ongoing state inside your body. Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, shows how trauma traps your nervous system in survival mode. Your body’s natural responses — fight, flight, freeze — get stuck or incomplete. When they don’t fully discharge, they turn into chronic tension and pain.

Imagine your nervous system as a complex communication network. Trauma jams the signals, causing your body to misinterpret harmless sensations as threats. Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory breaks down how your autonomic nervous system controls safety and danger responses. If your vagus nerve is out of balance, you experience pain and distress more intensely.

This isn’t mystical. It’s mechanical. Your nervous system’s wiring has changed. That’s why calming your nervous system is critical for healing chronic pain. It isn’t about just “thinking positive.” It’s about restoring physiological regulation.

Trauma-Informed Approaches to Healing Pain

Less theory. More practice. You can’t will your nervous system to calm down. You have to work with your body directly. Pat Ogden’s Sensorimotor Psychotherapy teaches you to notice what your body is feeling right now and to move through those sensations safely. This approach helps your nervous system complete the unfinished business of trauma and reduce pain.

Similarly, Resmaa Menakem’s work focusing on racialized trauma shows how pain and trauma manifest in specific parts of the body, like the hands or hips. He encourages somatic awareness and community-based healing as ways to dismantle trauma’s hold.

For anyone tackling chronic pain, a good starting point is to learn about your nervous system and to reconnect with your body without judgment. Books like The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy help decode the nervous system’s role in pain and trauma, making the complex understandable.

Why Traditional Treatments Often Miss the Mark

Many people with chronic pain feel like they’re caught in a revolving door of medical treatments that don’t address the root cause. Babette Rothschild points out that trauma-informed therapy often gets overlooked in pain management. The problem is that standard models look only at the body or only at the mind, never both together.

Bruce Perry’s neurosequential model emphasizes that trauma is stored in the body first. If you only treat symptoms — say, with painkillers or talk therapy alone — you’re patching a leak instead of fixing the pipe. You need interventions that integrate body and brain, like movement, breathwork, or somatic therapy.

Across the articles we’ve published on this site, we’ve consistently seen that addressing trauma somatically can reduce pain intensity and improve quality of life. It’s not quick or easy, but it’s real and measurable.

Getting Started: What Actually Works

Look, here’s the thing. You don’t have to wait for a perfect therapist or a miracle cure. Here’s what actually works for many people:

  • Body awareness practices that don't require mystical explanations, like gentle movement, breath regulation, or grounding exercises.
  • Somatic therapies that help your body complete those survival responses — shaking, stretching, or subtle movements that release tension.
  • Education about the nervous system so you understand what your body is doing and why.

If you want a practical guide, Waking the Tiger: Healing Trauma by Peter Levine remains a solid roadmap for working with trauma and its physical effects.

Also, small tools like grounding techniques or paced breathing can help regulate your nervous system daily — and make a big difference over time. You’re not broken. Your body just needs a chance to finish what it started.

Additional Resources

For those who want to explore the science behind trauma and chronic pain further, here’s a reliable source from the National Institutes of Health discussing the biopsychosocial model of chronic pain: NIH on Chronic Pain and Trauma.

Can trauma cause pain without injury?

Yes. Trauma can sensitize your nervous system so that even non-painful stimuli feel painful. This is called central sensitization. Your nervous system amplifies pain signals, creating chronic pain even when there’s no ongoing tissue damage.

How long does it take to heal trauma-related chronic pain?

There’s no set timeline. Healing depends on many factors like the severity of trauma, your current support system, and the methods you use. Consistent somatic work and nervous system regulation can help speed up recovery, but patience is essential.

What if I don’t remember my trauma?

You don’t have to consciously remember trauma for it to affect your body. Many traumatic experiences are stored non-verbally in your nervous system. Somatic therapies work directly with the body’s sensations, bypassing the need for detailed memory.

Chronic pain is your body’s way of holding onto unfinished business. You can’t always fix it with pills or talk therapy alone. You need to engage your body in the healing process, gently and consistently. The pain is real, but your nervous system’s response to trauma is what keeps it stuck.

So here’s my challenge: stop overthinking this. Start feeling — really feeling — what your body is trying to tell you. Find a movement or breath practice. Read a book like Anchored: How to Befriend Your Nervous System. Take one small step to resolve your body’s unfinished business. Your pain is waiting for that.

Reviewed by The Oracle Lover, Intuitive Educator and Oracle Guide. Last updated 2026-05-05.

title: Nervous System Regulation: Practical Tools for Daily Life
meta_description: Discover practical tools for nervous system regulation to support somatic trauma healing and improve daily emotional and physical well-being.
og_title: Nervous System Regulation: Practical Tools for Daily Life
og_description: Discover practical tools for nervous system regulation to support somatic trauma healing and improve daily emotional and physical well-being.
body:
Your nervous system controls how you feel and respond every day. Simple body-based tools can help you regulate it. This isn't mystical. It's mechanical, and you can practice it anytime.

Ever notice how stress can make your heart race or your hands shake, even when there’s no real danger? That’s your nervous system kicking into overdrive. The problem is, most of us don’t know how to calm it down. We wait for distractions or push through, hoping the feeling will pass. But what if you could actually control that response, right in the moment? What if that shaky, overwhelmed feeling didn’t have to run your life?

The Nervous System Isn’t a Mystery

Look, here’s the thing. The nervous system is often wrapped in woo or complicated science jargon. But it’s really just your body’s communication network. It decides if you’re safe or in danger. When it senses threat, it triggers fight, flight, or freeze responses. When it feels safe, it relaxes and helps your body repair. Stephen Porges, the scientist behind the Polyvagal Theory, showed how your vagus nerve acts like a brake on stress. Understanding this gives you a real way to calm down instead of spiraling out.

You don't have to be a neuroscientist to take advantage of how this works. When your nervous system is dysregulated—meaning stuck in stress mode—it impacts everything from your digestion to your mood. Over time, this can lead to chronic health issues. The good news? You can guide your nervous system back to balance with simple tools you use daily.

Breath: Your Most Accessible Regulator

Breathing is your direct line to the nervous system. It’s automatic, but you can control it. Pat Ogden, a pioneer in somatic psychology, emphasizes how intentional breathing regulates your body. When you slow your breath, especially with longer exhales, you activate your parasympathetic nervous system—the branch that tells your body “you’re safe.”

Try this right now: breathe in gently for 4 seconds, hold for 2, then breathe out for 6. Repeat a few times. You don’t have to think about it too much. This isn't mystical. It’s mechanical. The more you practice, the quicker your system learns to settle.

If you want to dig deeper, Anchored: How to Befriend Your Nervous System is a practical guide full of simple exercises that work.

Movement: Activate and Ground Your Body

Trauma therapist Bessel van der Kolk wrote in The Body Keeps the Score about how trauma gets "stuck" in the body. One way to release that is through movement. Not complicated yoga poses or intense workouts—just natural, gentle movement that reconnects you to your body.

This could be simple stretching, walking barefoot on grass, or shaking your arms out when you feel tense. Movement helps discharge excess energy your nervous system holds onto. Resmaa Menakem calls this “the racialized trauma in the body,” but the principle applies universally: movement grounds you in the present.

Over the years I’ve seen how clients who commit to small daily movement rituals feel less anxious and more present. It’s less about “fixing” and more about remembering you’re alive in your body.

Orientation: Engage Your Senses to Stay Present

When you feel overwhelmed, your nervous system is signaling you’re out of control. Dr. Dan Siegel talks about “mindsight,” the ability to observe your experience without getting swallowed by it. One way to develop this is through orientation—actively noticing your environment.

Here’s what actually works: look around and name five things you see, four things you can touch, three sounds you hear, two things you smell, and one thing you taste. This simple sensory check-in tells your brain you’re safe, grounded, and here. It shuts down the alarm bells in your nervous system.

Babette Rothschild, in her work on trauma, points out that orienting yourself to the present moment is a key step in calming the system. You don’t need hours of meditation. Even 30 seconds can interrupt a panic attack or intense stress.

Social Connection: It’s Not Just a Feeling

The body doesn’t lie. The mind does. Constantly. Your nervous system evolved for connection. When you feel seen and safe with someone else, your vagus nerve activates, and your system calms. This is why loneliness or isolation fuels anxiety.

Resmaa Menakem’s work shows that trauma isn’t just individual—it’s also collective. Healing happens in connection. This could be as simple as a genuine smile, a soft voice, or a touch on the arm. Stephen Porges calls this the “social engagement system.”

Try calling a trusted friend when you feel overwhelmed or even talking to yourself kindly in the mirror. It might sound odd, but it changes your nervous system’s state. Less theory. More practice.

Stop Overthinking This

You don’t need fancy equipment or years of study to calm your nervous system. The biggest mistake is thinking you have to figure it all out mentally. The body is always communicating. The question is whether you’re listening. Tools like breath, movement, orientation, and connection give you a direct line to that conversation.

If you want to explore more about trauma and the nervous system, check out this NIH article on somatic regulation and trauma. Science backs up what many healing traditions have said for centuries: calming your body calms your mind.

How long does it take to see results from nervous system regulation practices?

It varies. Some people feel shifts immediately with breathwork or grounding. Others need consistent daily practice over weeks to notice stable changes. The key is regularity, not perfection. Even a few minutes a day helps your system learn new patterns.

Can nervous system regulation help with anxiety and PTSD?

Yes, many trauma therapists, including Peter Levine who wrote Waking the Tiger: Healing Trauma, emphasize body-based techniques to manage PTSD symptoms. Regulating your nervous system reduces hyperarousal and helps you feel safer in your body.

Is nervous system regulation something everyone should learn?

Absolutely. Whether or not you've experienced trauma, stress hits everyone. Learning to regulate your nervous system improves sleep, focus, mood, and overall health. It’s a skill that benefits every aspect of your life.

Now it’s your turn. Pick one tool from this article and try it today. Breathe slower, move a little, orient your senses, or reach out. These small actions add up. You don’t have to wait for some perfect moment to start feeling better. Your nervous system is ready to listen.

Reviewed by The Oracle Lover, Intuitive Educator and Oracle Guide. Last updated 2026-05-05.

title: What Is Dorsal Vagal Shutdown and How Do You Come Back From It?
meta_description: Learn what dorsal vagal shutdown is, how it affects your body during trauma, and effective somatic healing techniques to regain balance and safety.
og_title: What Is Dorsal Vagal Shutdown and How Do You Come Back From It?
og_description: Learn what dorsal vagal shutdown is, how it affects your body during trauma, and effective somatic healing techniques to regain balance and safety.
body:
Dorsal vagal shutdown is your body’s extreme freeze response to overwhelming stress. It’s a survival mode, not a dysfunction. Healing requires gentle, body-based steps that help you reconnect with safety and aliveness.

Ever felt so numb, so disconnected, like you’ve checked out of your own life? That’s dorsal vagal shutdown. It’s not just feeling tired or drained. It’s your nervous system slamming on the brakes when it thinks you’re in mortal danger. Here’s the catch: you didn’t choose this. Your body did it to protect you. But staying there? That’s no way to live.

What Exactly Is Dorsal Vagal Shutdown?

Stephen Porges, the guy behind Polyvagal Theory, explains that our vagus nerve has two branches. The ventral vagal branch helps us connect socially and feel safe. The dorsal vagal branch is older, more primal. When things get too intense and the fight or flight options aren’t viable, the dorsal vagal system steps in. It essentially puts the body into a freeze or shutdown state.

This shutdown isn’t laziness or weakness. It’s your nervous system’s last-ditch effort to survive. Think of animals playing dead when threatened. Humans do this too, but in modern life, it often happens silently inside us. You might feel numb, disconnected, or emotionally flat. Sometimes, you literally feel like you’re fading out of your own body.

Bruce Perry, a psychiatrist specializing in trauma, calls this the “freeze” part of the fight-flight-freeze response. But dorsal vagal shutdown is more than just freezing in place. It’s a deep immobilization that slows your heart rate, lowers blood pressure, and can even reduce your body temperature.

Why Does Your Nervous System Do This?

Resmaa Menakem, a therapist focused on racialized trauma, points out that shutdown can happen when stress feels so overwhelming that fight or flight aren't options. Your brain perceives no safe way out, so it hits pause. This triggers survival biology. It’s not about choice or control. Your body just does what it needs to survive.

Look, here’s the thing. This isn’t mystical. It’s mechanical. When your sympathetic nervous system (fight/flight) maxes out and you can’t escape or defend, the dorsal vagal system activates to minimize harm. That means shutting down your sensory and emotional experience. The body conserves energy, the mind goes blank, and you might feel dissociated or depersonalized.

But this shutdown isn’t meant to last forever. It’s a temporary state. The problem is, trauma, chronic stress, or neglect can keep you trapped there. Over time, it rewires your nervous system, making you more prone to this shutdown even in less threatening situations.

How Do You Know If You’re in Dorsal Vagal Shutdown?

Bessel van der Kolk, author of The Body Keeps the Score (paid link), highlights symptoms like numbness, dissociation, low energy, and feeling “dead inside.” You might have trouble feeling joy or connecting with others. Your body feels heavy or frozen. You might also experience brain fog, memory issues, or a sense of hopelessness.

Babette Rothschild, another somatic trauma expert, warns that people in shutdown can easily misunderstand their experience as depression or fatigue. The difference is that dorsal vagal shutdown is a survival response, not just a mood disorder.

In my own practice, I’ve seen clients confuse shutdown with laziness or depression. Recognizing this physiological state is the first step to coming back online.

How Do You Come Back From Shutdown?

Stop overthinking this. Less theory. More practice. The nervous system doesn’t heal by thinking about it. It heals by feeling safe, here and now.

Start small. Stephen Porges suggests focusing on cues of safety—slow breathing, gentle touch, soft voices, warm environments. These help activate the ventral vagal system, your social engagement network, which calms the dorsal vagal freeze.

Peter Levine, author of Waking the Tiger: Healing Trauma (paid link), recommends somatic experiencing—tuning into bodily sensations to renegotiate stuck trauma rather than reliving it. You don't have to push or force. It’s about noticing where you feel “off,” then allowing your body to re-regulate naturally.

Babette Rothschild’s approach also highlights “pendulation”—the natural rhythm between activation and calm. You move gently between sensations of safety and distress, never overwhelming yourself. This builds nervous system resilience.

Something simple you can do right now is ground yourself. Feel your feet on the floor, notice your breath, or hold a comforting object. Tools like Anchored: How to Befriend Your Nervous System (paid link) offer practical exercises for this.

Over the years I’ve seen that body-based practices work best when combined with compassionate awareness. The body doesn’t lie. The mind does. Constantly. So trust your physical experience more than your runaway thoughts.

Remember, this isn’t about “fixing” yourself overnight. It’s about showing up for your body consistently, with patience and curiosity.

When Do You Need Professional Help?

If shutdown feels permanent or severely disrupts your life, it’s time to get support. Trauma specialists trained in somatic therapies can guide you through the process safely. Dan Siegel’s work on interpersonal neurobiology shows how safe relationships are the best medicine for healing nervous system trauma.

Research supports trauma therapy’s effectiveness too. For instance, a study indexed on PubMed highlights how somatic experiencing reduces PTSD symptoms by addressing body-based shutdown states.

Is dorsal vagal shutdown the same as dissociation?

Not exactly. Dissociation is a broader term describing feeling disconnected from yourself or reality. Dorsal vagal shutdown is a specific physiological state where your body freezes and numbs to protect you from extreme stress. Dissociation can happen with or without shutdown, but many people in shutdown experience dissociative symptoms.

Can breathing exercises help with dorsal vagal shutdown?

Yes, but gently. Slow, deep breathing can help activate the ventral vagal system and bring you back to safety. However, forcing deep breaths when you’re in shutdown might backfire. Start small, noticing your natural breath rhythm, and build from there.

How long does it take to recover from dorsal vagal shutdown?

There’s no set timeline. Recovery depends on your history, support system, and consistency with body-based practices. Some people start feeling shifts in days or weeks. For others, it takes months or years. The key is patience and steady connection with your body.

Here’s what actually works: acknowledging your shutdown without judgment, tuning into your sensations, and practicing gentle nervous system regulation consistently. Your nervous system is plastic. It can learn new patterns of safety. But you have to get curious and show up for your body.

So, next time you feel numb or checked out, don’t push harder. Instead, ask yourself: What’s my body trying to tell me? And then listen with kindness.

Reviewed by The Oracle Lover, Intuitive Educator and Oracle Guide. Last updated 2026-05-05.

title: Somatic Experiencing for Complex Trauma: What to Expect
meta_description: Discover how Somatic Experiencing helps heal complex trauma by releasing physical tension and restoring emotional balance. Learn what to expect in therapy.
og_title: Somatic Experiencing for Complex Trauma: What to Expect
og_description: Discover how Somatic Experiencing helps heal complex trauma by releasing physical tension and restoring emotional balance. Learn what to expect in therapy.
body:
Somatic Experiencing helps your nervous system finish what trauma interrupted. Complex trauma can feel overwhelming, but the body holds clues for healing. This isn't mystical. It's mechanical.

Imagine trying to fix a car engine blindfolded. You can hear it sputter, feel it stall, but you have no idea what’s wrong. That’s what complex trauma feels like inside your body. You sense something’s off, but you can’t quite name it. Somatic Experiencing (SE) is like removing that blindfold. It tunes you into how your body stores trauma and gives you tools to reset your nervous system. But if you’re expecting magic or instant relief, prepare for a different story. This is about rewiring your body’s survival responses step-by-step, sometimes slowly, but always with results.

What Is Somatic Experiencing and Why Does It Matter for Complex Trauma?

Somatic Experiencing was developed by Peter Levine, a trauma therapist with a background in biology and psychology. His key insight was that trauma isn’t stored as memories alone but as physiological responses trapped in the nervous system. This is especially true for complex trauma—repeated, prolonged trauma like childhood abuse, neglect, or ongoing stress. The body gets stuck in a state of hyperarousal or shutdown, unable to complete the natural defensive responses it started but never finished.

In plain terms: your nervous system is stuck in fight, flight, or freeze mode, even when danger is long gone. SE helps you track those physical sensations, gently nudging your system to complete the incomplete responses and regulate itself. The goal is not to relive trauma but to renegotiate how your body reacts to triggers.

Stop overthinking this. The body doesn’t lie. The mind does. Constantly.

How Does SE Work? Less Theory. More Practice.

Instead of diving into endless talk therapy, Somatic Experiencing focuses on felt sense—the physical feelings inside your body. Think tingling, tightness, heat, or heaviness. Your therapist will guide you to notice these sensations without pushing or forcing. It’s about slow, manageable tracking that builds your nervous system’s capacity to handle stress.

Babette Rothschild, a trauma expert, writes about the importance of titration in trauma work. That means working in small doses, so you don’t overwhelm your system. SE practitioners use this principle to keep you grounded and safe. You might start with noticing subtle shifts in your body, like your feet on the floor or your breath moving in and out. Over time, you learn to “ride the wave” of sensations instead of blocking or avoiding them.

The process includes pendulation—moving back and forth between states of activation and calm. This back-and-forth lets your nervous system learn it can return to safety after arousal. It’s like training a muscle. You build resilience by practicing regulation in small increments.

In my own practice, I’ve seen clients surprised at how powerful simply noticing physical sensations can be. No dramatic reliving of traumatic events, just steady, patient presence with whatever is happening inside.

What to Expect in Sessions: Safety, Patience, and Practical Steps

Look, here’s the thing. SE is not a quick fix. Complex trauma has rewired your nervous system over years. It takes time to rewire it again. Your sessions will likely start slow. The therapist may ask you to describe sensations or notice parts of your body you usually ignore. You won’t be forced to recount traumatic stories in detail or dive into memories unless you want to.

The focus is on safety and regulation. The therapist helps you find where you feel “okay” in your body, then gently explores sensations around trauma without flooding you. They might use touch, movement, or breath exercises, always checking your tolerance. You learn to recognize when you’re getting overwhelmed and how to return to calm.

Stephen Porges’s Polyvagal Theory supports this approach. It explains how the vagus nerve controls your body's state of calm or alarm. SE sessions work to strengthen your "social engagement system," helping you feel safer in your body and relationships.

If you’re curious about the science behind this, check out this PubMed article on somatic trauma therapies. It highlights how body-based approaches aid nervous system regulation.

Tools You Can Use Between Sessions

SE is not just about what happens in therapy. You also practice between sessions. Grounding exercises, breath work, and mindful movement can build your capacity for regulation. Resmaa Menakem, in his book My Grandmother's Hands, emphasizes the importance of somatic awareness for racialized trauma, but his grounding techniques apply broadly.

For example, simple practices like feeling your feet on the ground, gently rocking your body, or slow diaphragmatic breathing can shift your nervous system from alarm to calm. Bruce Perry, a leading child trauma expert, shows how rhythm and patterned movement create safety.

Try these resources for deepening your practice:

  • The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk
  • Anchored: How to Befriend Your Nervous System by Deb Dana
  • In an Unspoken Voice by Peter Levine

Stop overthinking this. The practice is in the doing, not theorizing.

Common Questions About Somatic Experiencing for Complex Trauma

Is Somatic Experiencing safe for people with severe trauma?

Yes. SE is designed to be gentle and paced according to your nervous system’s tolerance. Therapists use titration—small doses of sensation—to avoid overwhelm. However, it’s important to work with a trained SE professional who understands complex trauma.

Will I have to talk about my trauma stories in detail?

No. SE focuses on body sensations rather than detailed narrative recall. You may discuss feelings or sensations, but you won’t be pressured to relive traumatic memories. The aim is to process trauma through the body, not the mind alone.

How long does healing take with SE?

It varies greatly depending on your trauma history, nervous system resilience, and consistency of practice. Complex trauma often means years of dysregulation, so patience is key. Many clients notice improvements in regulation and presence within months, but full resolution takes time.

Final Thoughts: Healing Is a Journey, Not a Destination

Complex trauma doesn’t resolve overnight. The nervous system has its own timeline. Somatic Experiencing isn’t a magic bullet. It’s a practical, body-based way of reclaiming your nervous system’s natural ability to regulate and feel safe. It asks you to be patient with your body and yourself.

If you’re tired of mental gymnastics and want to stop running from your own body, SE offers a grounded path forward. Remember, this isn’t mystical. It’s mechanical. The body knows how to heal when given the right conditions.

Try small steps daily. Notice your sensations. Ride the wave of activation and calm. You’re not broken—you’re simply holding unfinished business. And you can finish it.

Reviewed by The Oracle Lover, Intuitive Educator and Oracle Guide. Last updated 2026-05-05.

title: The Body Scan: A Somatic Practice for Reconnecting With Your Body
meta_description: Discover how the Body Scan somatic practice helps heal trauma by increasing body awareness and reconnecting you with physical sensations.
og_title: The Body Scan: A Somatic Practice for Reconnecting With Your Body
og_description: Discover how the Body Scan somatic practice helps heal trauma by increasing body awareness and reconnecting you with physical sensations.
body:
Body scans help you reconnect to sensations you might be ignoring. This somatic practice grounds you in your physical experience to reduce stress and trauma symptoms. The body doesn’t lie. The mind does. Constantly.

When was the last time you actually felt your body? Not just spotted your hands or feet but truly experienced the sensation of existence from head to toe? If you’re like most people carrying trauma or chronic stress, your body has become a strange, unfamiliar place. You’re dissociated, numb, or constantly “in your head.” That’s where the body scan comes in. It’s not some woo-woo trick. This isn’t mystical. It’s mechanical. It’s a simple, evidence-based practice that helps you reestablish a direct line of communication with yourself.

What Is a Body Scan and Why Does It Matter?

A body scan is a somatic practice where you systematically bring your attention to different parts of your body, noticing sensations without judgment. It’s not about fixing anything or trying to feel good instantly. It’s about noticing what’s there, in real time. Peter Levine, who pioneered somatic experiencing, often emphasizes that trauma isn’t stored in your story or head—it’s locked in your body’s nervous system. The body scan creates a bridge back to your physical reality, helping you notice sensations that have been suppressed or ignored.

Look, here’s the thing. You might think you’re aware of your body, but trauma and chronic stress change your nervous system so deeply that parts of you become anesthetized. This practice helps you catch those blind spots. It’s less theory. More practice. You’re retraining your brain’s old habit of bypassing uncomfortable feelings.

The Science Behind Feeling Your Body

Stephen Porges, who developed the Polyvagal Theory, explains how our nervous system reacts to danger by shutting down or hyperactivating. During a body scan, you can start to identify which state you’re in. Are you tense in your shoulders? Is your breath shallow? That unconscious tension is a clue your nervous system is either stuck in fight, flight, or freeze.

By slowly moving your attention through your body, you introduce a kind of “soft curiosity.” Over time, this process helps regulate your autonomic nervous system. It’s like telling your body, “I see you. You’re safe now.” This rewiring is backed by research showing how mindfulness-based somatic practices reduce symptoms of PTSD and anxiety (see this study on mindfulness and trauma).

How to Do a Body Scan (Without Overthinking It)

Stop overthinking this. You don’t need a fancy retreat or special equipment. You can do a body scan anywhere, anytime. Here’s what actually works:

  1. Find a quiet space where you won’t be interrupted. Sit or lie down comfortably.
  2. Start by taking a few deep breaths. Notice the air moving in and out of your lungs.
  3. Bring your attention to your feet. Feel the weight of them against the floor or surface.
  4. Slowly move your focus upward: ankles, calves, knees, thighs. Notice any tightness, warmth, tingling, or absence of sensation.
  5. If your mind wanders, gently bring it back without judgment.
  6. Continue scanning your torso, arms, neck, and finally your head.
  7. End by taking a few slow breaths and noticing your body as a whole.

Babette Rothschild, a trauma therapist who writes extensively on somatic interventions, recommends that you approach each part with open curiosity. Don’t force sensations. Just notice whatever is present, even if it’s “nothing.”

In my own practice, clients often report that this simple method reduces their chronic tension and anxiety after just a few sessions. The key is consistency, not intensity.

Common Challenges and How to Handle Them

Many people find the body scan uncomfortable at first. Trauma survivors might feel overwhelmed by sensations or disconnected from their bodies entirely. Resmaa Menakem, author of My Grandmother’s Hands (paid link), highlights how racialized trauma can create unique barriers to body awareness, making this practice harder but even more essential.

Here’s what you can do if you get stuck:

  • If tensions or emotions feel too intense, shorten your scan and focus only on a small area.
  • Use grounding techniques in between, like pressing your feet firmly on the floor or holding a cold object.
  • Try pairing the scan with another somatic method like slow movement or breathwork. Anchored: How to Befriend Your Nervous System (paid link) has great options for this.

Bruce Perry, a leading expert on trauma neurobiology, says that pacing is everything. You can’t rush healing or body reconnection. It happens one small piece at a time.

Why You Should Read These Books Alongside Your Practice

Want to understand the science and stories behind the body scan and somatic healing? Check out these classics:

  • The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk. This book is the gold standard for understanding trauma’s impact on the body and brain.
  • Waking the Tiger: Healing Trauma by Peter Levine. A practical guide to somatic experiencing and how trauma manifests physically.
  • The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy by Stephen Porges. For a deep dive into nervous system science and clinical applications.

Across the articles we’ve published on this site, the consistent message is clear: the body doesn’t lie. The mind does. Constantly. If you want lasting change, you have to start by feeling where you are now.

How long should a body scan take?

It can take anywhere from 5 to 45 minutes. Beginners might start with 5-10 minutes and gradually increase the time as they get more comfortable. The important thing is regular practice, not the duration.

Can I do a body scan if I have severe trauma or PTSD?

Yes, but with caution. If you have severe trauma, it’s best to work with a trauma-informed therapist who can guide you safely. You might start with very brief scans or focus on grounding techniques before expanding your practice.

Is the body scan a meditation?

It shares similarities with mindfulness meditation but focuses specifically on bodily sensations rather than thoughts or emotions. It’s a somatic practice that complements meditation but is distinct in its intent and method.

Conclusion: Stop Waiting to Feel Whole

Feeling disconnected from your body isn’t a personal failure. It’s a survival adaptation. But that doesn’t mean you have to stay that way. The body scan is a straightforward, science-backed way to start reconnecting. Less theory. More practice. It’s uncomfortable, sometimes hard, but it’s doable. Remember: your nervous system is always listening, even when your mind isn’t. So why not start sending it some friendly signals? Try a body scan today. Feel yourself. You might be surprised what shows up.

Reviewed by The Oracle Lover, Intuitive Educator and Oracle Guide. Last updated 2026-05-05.

title: Titration in Trauma Therapy: Why Slow Is Actually Faster
meta_description: Discover how titration in trauma therapy helps heal somatic trauma effectively by pacing exposure to triggers for faster, lasting recovery.
og_title: Titration in Trauma Therapy: Why Slow Is Actually Faster
og_description: Discover how titration in trauma therapy helps heal somatic trauma effectively by pacing exposure to triggers for faster, lasting recovery.
body:
Titration slows down trauma healing so you don’t get overwhelmed. Small doses of activation help your nervous system reorganize safely. Fast breakthroughs feel good but often backfire—slow is actually faster in lasting recovery.

Imagine trying to empty a flooded basement by dumping buckets of water into the street all at once. You’d probably just create a mess that flows back in faster than you can scoop it out. Trauma therapy works the same way. You might think going full throttle speeds healing—but pushing too hard can retraumatize you, setting you back. Here’s the counterintuitive truth: slow is actually faster.

What Is Titration and Why Does It Matter?

Titration is a fancy word for “going slow.” In trauma therapy, it means introducing trauma-related material in tiny, manageable doses. Instead of diving headfirst into painful memories or sensations, you inch forward, allowing your nervous system to adjust at its own pace. The goal is to avoid flooding your system with too much activation, which can cause shutdown, dissociation, or retraumatization.

Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, popularized this approach. He observed that animals in the wild rarely get stuck in trauma because they discharge energy gradually instead of reliving the entire event all at once. Levine’s work shows that slow pacing helps your nervous system complete that natural discharge process so trauma doesn’t get trapped.

Stop overthinking this. The body doesn’t lie. The mind does. Constantly. Your nervous system will signal when it’s ready for more. Learning to listen to those signals instead of bulldozing through them is key.

The Neuroscience of Slow Healing

Dr. Stephen Porges, creator of the Polyvagal Theory, helps us understand the nervous system’s role in trauma recovery. According to Porges, your body’s autonomic nervous system (ANS) has three states: safe/social, fight/flight, and freeze/shutdown. Trauma pushes your system into fight/flight or freeze states, often repeatedly.

Trying to push through trauma too fast can keep your ANS stuck in hyperarousal or dissociation. Titration respects the ANS’s rhythm by providing just enough stimulus to shift your state gently from danger to safety. Your nervous system begins to “retune,” and over time you develop more resilience and regulation.

Babette Rothschild, an expert in trauma and somatic therapy, emphasizes titration as a practical tool to access traumatic memories without overwhelming the body. She says, “Too much too fast and you risk retraumatizing. Too little, and no progress happens. Titration seeks the sweet spot.”

Why Fast Breakthroughs Often Backfire

In the trauma world, there’s a temptation to race for big breakthroughs. You might be drawn to intense emotional releases or cathartic reliving sessions. It feels good to finally “let it out,” but here’s what actually works: slow pacing prevents these breakthroughs from collapsing into chaos.

Bruce Perry’s research on child trauma shows that when the brain experiences overwhelming stress, it can dissociate or shut down parts of itself. If therapy goes too fast, it can push you right into this shutdown state. That means no real integration happens, and trauma remains locked in place.

In my own practice, I’ve seen clients who tried quick fixes end up retraumatized, stuck in cycles of intense symptoms that get worse before they get better. Titration, on the other hand, steadies the nervous system so healing is progressive and lasting.

Titration in Practice: What Does It Look Like?

Look, here’s the thing. Titration isn’t about tiptoeing forever. It’s about intentional pacing and somatic tracking. That means noticing bodily sensations that arise with trauma-related material, then pausing before things escalate. You might spend sessions just noticing tightness in your chest or a flutter in your gut, without pushing to “fix” it immediately.

Pat Ogden’s Sensorimotor Psychotherapy is a great example of this. The therapist guides you to become aware of subtle shifts in your body, helping you build tolerance for those sensations over time. This process strengthens your capacity for self-regulation and reduces trauma’s grip.

For hands-on support, consider resources like The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) by Bessel van der Kolk. It explains how trauma lives in the body and why slow, somatic approaches are essential.

The Science Supports It

Research on titration shows that smaller doses of trauma activation produce more stable outcomes. For instance, a 2017 study in the Journal of Traumatic Stress found that pacing trauma exposure reduced drop-out rates and improved symptom reduction compared to massed exposure.

Less theory. More practice. The research aligns with clinical wisdom: your nervous system can only handle so much at once. Titration respects that limit. It’s not mystical. It’s mechanical.

If you want to get practical tools for befriending your nervous system, check out Anchored: How to Befriend Your Nervous System (paid link). It’s full of exercises you can use to build safety in your body.

Common Questions About Titration

How do I know if I’m going too fast in trauma therapy?

If you feel overwhelmed, dissociated, or stuck in intense emotional loops after sessions, that’s a warning sign. Your body might feel frozen or hyperaroused for days. If that happens, talk to your therapist about slowing down and focusing on somatic regulation instead of trauma content.

Can titration be done without a therapist?

You can start small by practicing mindfulness of bodily sensations and learning to notice when you’re triggered. But trauma therapy is safer with a skilled guide. If you try working alone, keep doses very small and prioritize safety. Books like Waking the Tiger (paid link) by Peter Levine are excellent self-study options.

How long does titration-based therapy take?

It varies by individual. Some people see progress in months, others need years. Remember, the goal isn’t speed but stability. The slower you go, the less chance of setbacks, and the more lasting your healing will be.

Wrapping It Up: Slow Down to Speed Up

You might want to sprint through trauma work, but your nervous system has its own timetable. Titration honors that rhythm by breaking down big trauma storms into manageable raindrops. Over time, those small drops add up to a flood of healing that sticks. The next time you’re tempted to charge full speed ahead, remember: slow is actually faster.

Across the articles we’ve published on this site, consistent pacing and somatic awareness come up again and again as keys to real recovery. So don’t rush. Trust your body’s signals. Engage with your nervous system patiently, and watch your healing unfold with lasting results.

Reviewed by The Oracle Lover, Intuitive Educator and Oracle Guide. Last updated 2026-05-05.

title: Internal Family Systems and the Body: Parts Work Meets Somatic Healing
meta_description: Explore how Internal Family Systems integrates with somatic healing to address trauma by connecting mind and body through parts work techniques.
og_title: Internal Family Systems and the Body: Parts Work Meets Somatic Healing
og_description: Explore how Internal Family Systems integrates with somatic healing to address trauma by connecting mind and body through parts work techniques.
body:
Internal Family Systems (IFS) therapy works directly with body sensations. Trauma lives in your body as much as in your mind. Combining IFS with somatic healing offers practical ways to heal and regulate your nervous system.

Look, here’s the thing. You can’t separate your mind from your body when it comes to trauma. You might sit on a therapist’s couch, talk about your feelings, and still feel stuck. Why? Because trauma is stored in the nervous system, in muscle tightness, in breath patterns, and in the very cells of your body. Internal Family Systems, or IFS, has been around for decades as a way to talk to your inner "parts." But what if you took those parts work conversations and brought them into your body? What if healing wasn’t just about talking, but about feeling and moving too?

What Is Internal Family Systems? It’s Not Just Head Stuff

Developed by Richard Schwartz in the 1980s, IFS treats the mind like a system of different "parts," each with its own perspective, memories, and feelings. You have Protectors that try to shield you from pain, Exiles that hold trauma, and a core Self that’s calm and curious. The goal is to get your Self to lead the inner team, helping protectors relax and exiles heal. This theory might sound abstract, but it’s grounded in real clinical work and research.

Over the years I’ve seen how IFS gives people a framework to stop fighting internally and start listening. But here’s the kicker: the internal parts don’t just live in your head. They live in your body. The tension in your chest might be a protector yelling to keep you safe. The flutter in your stomach might be an exile begging for attention. When you bring your body into the mix, the therapy becomes whole.

Trauma and the Body: The Science of Somatic Healing

Trauma is not just a memory; it’s a biological event. Dr. Bessel van der Kolk’s research in The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) shows that trauma rewires your nervous system, making it hypervigilant or shut down. Your body remembers what your mind forgets or refuses to face. That’s why somatic therapies focus on sensations, movement, and breathing to reset your nervous system.

Peter Levine’s Waking the Tiger (paid link) emphasizes the fight-flight-freeze response stored in the body. When you engage with your feelings through your senses—not just your thoughts—you start releasing stuck energy. This release is critical to healing trauma.

So, the question is: What happens when you bring parts work into your body? How do you listen to those protector parts or exiled feelings that show up as tightness, pain, or numbness? This is where somatic healing and IFS meet.

How IFS and Somatic Healing Work Together

Babette Rothschild, a somatic therapist and trauma expert, points out that trauma symptoms often show up as physical sensations long before you can talk about them. When you combine IFS with somatic awareness, you don’t just “talk to” your parts—you feel them.

Imagine noticing a knot in your throat or a heavy weight in your chest. Instead of ignoring that or trying to push it away, you ask, “Which part of me is feeling this? What’s it trying to protect me from?” Then you listen with curiosity instead of judgment. That’s less theory, more practice.

Stephen Porges’s Polyvagal Theory (explained well in The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy paid link) helps here by explaining how your nervous system shifts between states: safe, mobilized, or immobilized. Certain parts might get stuck in fight or freeze modes. Body sensations tell you where you are. Your Self’s job is to calm those parts by gently engaging with their sensations and stories.

Practical Steps to Bring Parts Work Into Your Body

Stop overthinking this. Here’s what actually works:

  1. Identify the sensation. Close your eyes. Scan your body. Where do you feel tension, heat, cooling, or pressure? Note it without trying to change it.
  2. Name the part. Ask yourself, “Which part is this? A protector, an exile, or maybe a manager?” You don’t have to be perfect. Just try.
  3. Ask with curiosity. “What are you afraid will happen if you relax? What do you want me to know?” Let the body’s story unfold, even if it’s just a feeling or image.
  4. Breathe into the sensation. Use slow, deep breathing to invite your nervous system to calm. Repeat if needed.
  5. Invite the Self. Imagine your calm and compassionate core reaching out to that part. Sit with the sensations and feelings without pushing them away.

In my own practice, clients who combine IFS with somatic techniques report faster shifts. The body doesn’t lie. The mind does. Constantly.

Why Science Supports This Approach

Studies on body-focused therapies show changes in brain areas involved in emotion regulation, like the prefrontal cortex and amygdala. Dr. Dan Siegel emphasizes integration between different brain regions as key to healing. Parts work helps by giving voice to hidden feelings, and somatic awareness helps by physically regulating states. Together, they improve emotional resilience.

Resmaa Menakem’s My Grandmother’s Hands (paid link) highlights the importance of racialized trauma stored in the body, reminding us that trauma healing must address both mind and body carefully and respectfully.

If you want to go deeper, the NIH hosts plenty of research on somatic therapies and IFS in trauma treatment. For example, this study on IFS and PTSD shows promising results.

Can I practice IFS with somatic techniques on my own?

Yes, you can start on your own by tuning into body sensations and naming inner parts. However, trauma work can bring up strong emotions or memories. If you feel overwhelmed, reach out to a trained IFS or somatic therapist.

How long does it take to see results combining IFS and somatic healing?

It depends on your trauma history, support system, and commitment. Some people notice shifts in a few weeks; others may take months or longer. Consistency and patience matter more than speed.

What if I don’t feel anything in my body during parts work?

That’s normal. Some people are disconnected from bodily sensations due to trauma or habit. Start with small awareness exercises such as mindful breathing or gentle movement. Over time, the connection will grow.

Stop overthinking this. Healing is not about getting it right on the first try. It’s about showing up for yourself again and again.

If you want to start your journey, consider reading The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) by Bessel van der Kolk. It’s a classic that bridges mind and body trauma work perfectly.

Here’s the challenge: next time you feel stress, tightness, or a sudden emotion, pause. Ask yourself who’s feeling that inside you. Don’t just analyze. Feel. Breathe. Talk to that part. Your body has answers. You just need to listen.

Reviewed by The Oracle Lover, Intuitive Educator and Oracle Guide. Last updated 2026-05-05.

title: How to Find a Somatic Therapist: What to Look For and What to Ask
meta_description: Learn how to find a somatic therapist for trauma healing with clear tips on what to look for and essential questions to ask before starting therapy.
og_title: How to Find a Somatic Therapist: What to Look For and What to Ask
og_description: Learn how to find a somatic therapist for trauma healing with clear tips on what to look for and essential questions to ask before starting therapy.
body:
Finding a somatic therapist means knowing what somatic work really is. Trust your body and the therapist’s experience, not just fancy certifications. Ask clear questions about their approach and how they handle trauma in the body.

Look, here’s the thing. Trauma lives in your body first, long before words show up. You might feel stuck, restless, or disconnected, and talk therapy alone hasn’t gotten you where you want. So, you’re thinking about somatic therapy. But how do you find the right person? There’s no simple directory that guarantees a good match. It’s overwhelming to sift through jargon and buzzwords. You want someone who knows the body doesn’t lie. The mind does. Constantly.

What Somatic Therapy Actually Means

Somatic therapy isn’t some new-age fad. It’s based on the fact that trauma changes your nervous system and body sensations. Dr. Bessel van der Kolk, author of The Body Keeps the Score, showed decades ago how trauma’s imprint is stored in the body’s muscles, organs, and nervous system—not just your brain’s memory banks.

So, if a therapist calls themselves somatic but only talks, that’s a red flag. You want someone skilled at noticing and working with bodily sensations: tension, breath, movement, posture. It’s less about theory and more about practice. Dr. Peter Levine, who wrote Waking the Tiger: Healing Trauma, developed exercises that help you complete the body’s natural responses to trauma, like shaking off a threat. This isn’t mystical. It’s mechanical.

Licensing and Training: What Really Matters

You’ll find somatic therapy practitioners come from many backgrounds: psychology, social work, bodywork, massage, physical therapy, or counseling. Licensing doesn’t always tell you if they’re good with somatic trauma work.

Look instead for specialized training in somatic trauma approaches. For example, Pat Ogden’s Sensorimotor Psychotherapy certificate, or Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory-informed therapy (see The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy). These are frameworks proven in peer-reviewed studies to help regulate the nervous system and restore safety in the body.

Over the years I’ve seen therapists with years of general therapy experience but little real somatic training struggle to hold clients safely in their body’s nervous system shifts. Trauma is not just psychological; it’s biological. Make sure your therapist knows that.

Questions to Ask Before You Commit

Stop overthinking this. When you talk to a prospective therapist, ask clear, direct questions. Here’s what actually works:

  • What is your training and experience with somatic trauma therapy? Ask about specific certifications, workshops, or mentorships focused on somatic approaches.
  • How do you work with the body during sessions? You want them to describe specific techniques like breath work, movement, body sensing, or titration (going slow through triggers), not just “talk therapy.”
  • How do you ensure safety when working with trauma in the body? Trauma work can activate intense sensations or memories. A good therapist has clear strategies to keep you grounded and in control.
  • Can you work with dissociation or flashbacks? These are common in trauma survivors, and not every therapist is trained to handle them somatically.
  • What can I expect in a typical session? This helps you imagine if the therapist’s style fits your needs.

The body doesn’t lie. The mind does. Constantly. A therapist who respects that fact will be transparent and practical with their answers.

Trust Your Gut and Your Body’s Signals

It’s tempting to pick a therapist based on glowing reviews or a fancy website. But your body will tell you what’s right if you pay attention. In my own practice, I’ve noticed clients often know within minutes if a therapist feels safe or not. That’s because somatic therapy is about connection on a physical and emotional level.

Do you feel heard? Seen? Safe enough to relax your muscles and breathe? Notice your body’s responses after and during the first session. If you feel more tense, confused, or triggered without support, keep looking.

Resources to Explore Somatic Therapy Further

Before you start, it helps to build a little foundation. I recommend reading foundational texts like In an Unspoken Voice by Peter Levine or Anchored: How to Befriend Your Nervous System by Deb Dana. These books provide clear language about how trauma lives in your body and how healing can happen.

If you want to understand the science behind trauma healing, this NIH article on somatic experiencing offers a peer-reviewed perspective on techniques that help regulate the nervous system.

Remember, less theory. More practice. The true test of a somatic therapist is whether they can help you reconnect with your body in a way that feels safe and freeing.

How do I know if somatic therapy is right for me?

If you feel stuck in your body, have symptoms like chronic tension, anxiety, or dissociation, and traditional talk therapy hasn’t helped fully, somatic therapy might be a good fit. It’s especially helpful if you’re willing to engage with body sensations and movement as part of healing.

Can somatic therapy work alongside other therapies?

Absolutely. Somatic therapy often complements traditional psychotherapy, medication, or other healing practices. The goal is to integrate the body’s wisdom with the mind’s work for deeper, lasting healing.

What if I don’t feel comfortable with touch or movement?

Somatic therapy is not about forcing anything physical you don’t want. Good therapists tailor sessions to your comfort level. They may use breath, guided awareness, or subtle movement instead of hands-on work. Always communicate your boundaries clearly.

Finding the right somatic therapist isn’t about luck or fancy certifications. It’s about clarity on what somatic therapy really is, asking practical questions, and trusting your body’s response to the therapist’s presence. The body doesn’t lie; the mind does. Constantly.

So here’s the challenge: take one step today. Reach out to a therapist who mentions somatic trauma work. Ask the questions above. Notice how you feel before, during, and after the session. Keep your focus on your body’s cues. Healing happens there.

You deserve to feel safe, connected, and alive in your own skin. Don’t settle for less.

Reviewed by The Oracle Lover, Intuitive Educator and Oracle Guide. Last updated 2026-05-05.
